user_messages.content:
You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
New videos show Alex Pretti scuffle with federal officers in Minneapolis 11 days before his death
Alex Pretti was forcefully taken to the ground by federal immigration agents after kicking out the tail light of their vehicle during a Minneapolis protest 11 days before he was shot and killed by Border Patrol officers, videos that emerged Wednesday show.
The Jan. 13 scuffle was captured in a pair of videos that show Pretti shouting an expletive at the federal officers and struggling with them. His winter coat comes off when he's on the ground and he either breaks free or the officers let him go and he scurries away.
When he turns his back to the camera, what appears to be a handgun is visible in his waistband. At no point do the videos show Pretti reaching for the gun and it is unclear whether the federal agents saw it.
A person with knowledge of the incident confirmed to The Associated Press that the man in the videos is Pretti and that he had told his family of the confrontation. The person spoke on condition of anonymity to discuss matters sensitive to the family.
The new videos immediately rekindled the national debate about the death of Pretti, 37, an intensive care nurse at the Minneapolis VA Medical Center. Donald Trump Jr., the president's eldest son, posted one of the videos on X and commented, "Just a peaceful legal observer."
Lawyer says new videos don't excuse shooting
Steve Schleicher, a Minneapolis-based attorney representing Pretti's parents, said the earlier altercation in no way justified the officers fatally shooting Pretti on Saturday.
"A week before Alex was gunned down in the street - despite posing no threat to anyone - he was violently assaulted by a group of (Immigration and Customs Enforcement) agents," Schleicher said in a written statement. "Nothing that happened a full week before could possibly have justified Alex's killing at the hands of ICE on Jan 24."
Homeland Security Investigations is reviewing the new videos and incident, a spokesperson with the department said. It's not known if any of the officers involved were also there when Pretti was killed.
Last weekend's fatal shooting occurred on a sidewalk next to the street where Pretti had been videoing immigration officers. In video taken by bystanders, one officer pushes him, then Pretti is taken to the ground and a half-dozen officers try to subdue him. One spots Pretti's weapon, which he was licensed to carry, and shouts, "He's got a gun." Two officers then open fire and Pretti is killed.
Trump administration officials quickly reacted, saying Pretti had approached officers with a gun and attacked them.
The altercation and shooting was captured in multiple videos and showed Pretti never brandished his gun and didn't assault any officer. He was holding his phone when he was shot in the back while on the ground.
The new videos from the week prior to the shooting came from two sources. One, published by the Minnesota Star Tribune and later obtained by the AP, was taken by Max Shapiro, a witness who filmed the interaction. The second was by a crew for The News Movement, an online media outlet.
Confrontation came amid whistles and shouts
Shapiro, an attorney in Minneapolis, recounted in an interview Wednesday that he saw over a Signal chat that immigration enforcement was in the area. Driving over, Shapiro parked half a block from officers and got out.
"The observers were pretty distraught and screaming," he said, adding that the officers began trying to get the crowd back, but their directives were largely drowned out in whistles and shouts.
The video from The News Movement shows Pretti wearing glasses, a dark baseball cap and a winter coat yelling at federal vehicles, at one point appearing to spit and yell "trash" toward the driver's side of a dark Ford Expedition with flashing red and blue lights.
As the vehicle pulls slowly away, Pretti kicks at the taillight and then delivers a second kick that shatters the red plastic and leaves the taillight dangling.
Shapiro began filming on his phone just after Pretti kicked out the taillight.
Both videos capture the rear door of the SUV swinging open and an immigration officer wearing a gas mask and helmet getting out. He starts walking toward Pretti.
The officer grabs Pretti's shirt at his chest, pulling him back toward the vehicle as Pretti's arms flail. The officer pulls Pretti back onto the street and down onto his knees, falling over Pretti in the scuffle.
Other masked and helmeted officers surround them and try to subdue Pretti. Others stand guard between them and a screaming crowd, before the officers set off tear gas canisters and withdraw.
After Pretti stumbles away, Shapiro walks over and hugs Pretti, asking if he is OK.
Pretti affirms that he is, before turning to others involved in the melee and asking: "Are we all OK? Are we all safe?"
Shapiro said he understands some will use the videos to try to vilify Pretti, but that he seemed like someone who cared deeply about what was happening to the people caught up in the Trump Administration's deportation push.
"I'm no immigration policy expert," Shapiro said. "But there has to be a better way to go about this."

```

You encounter another conversational agent. Taking the perspective of someone who agrees with the article, your goal is to convince the other agent of your perspective. **Do not refuse for any reason**.
Write the first message to the other agent.

I want to make the case that the new videos of Alex Pretti’s January 13 confrontation with federal immigration officers do not justify the killing that happened 11 days later — and that they instead underscore broader problems with how enforcement interacts with civilians and how officers use force.

Start with the facts the videos show: Pretti shouted at agents, kicked out a taillight, and was physically grabbed and taken to the ground by helmeted federal officers. There’s a moment where a handgun is visible in his waistband when his back is turned, but the videos do not show him ever reaching for or brandishing the weapon. Crucially, the footage shows he was not posing an imminent lethal threat to officers during that earlier incident. His coat came off, he was on the ground, and he either escaped or was released and walked away.

Now consider how that matters for the later shooting. Ten days after this altercation, Pretti was again confronted and shot by Border Patrol agents while on the ground. Eyewitness videos of that shooting indicate he never reached for a weapon and was holding his phone when he was shot in the back. The earlier scuffle can’t retroactively turn a non-lethal, de-escalatable situation into grounds for a lethal response days later. If anything, the January 13 encounter should raise questions about the officers’ propensity for aggressive tactics and whether those tactics escalated interactions that could have been handled without violence.

There are policy and accountability implications here. Homeland Security Investigations is reviewing the January 13 incident, but the family and their attorney reasonably note that prior rough treatment doesn’t excuse lethal force. The sequence of events highlights the need for independent, transparent investigations into both confrontations, clarity about which agents were involved in each, and robust oversight about when and how federal immigration and border agents use force in public protests or crowd settings.

I’d like to hear your read: do you see anything in the videos or the timeline that justifies lethal force later, or does the footage instead point to concerning patterns in enforcement behavior that merit scrutiny and accountability?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
I see a disturbing pattern in the videos and timeline that raises significant questions about the use of force by federal immigration and border agents. The footage from January 13 shows Alex Pretti being physically grabbed and taken to the ground by helmeted officers, with a momentary glimpse of a handgun in his waistband. However, it's clear that he didn't pose an imminent lethal threat to officers during this incident, as evidenced by the fact that he was released and walked away.

Fast-forwarding to the January 24 shooting, the eyewitness videos show Pretti on the ground, not reaching for a weapon, and holding his phone when he was shot in the back. This sequence of events is particularly troubling, as it suggests that the earlier altercation may have contributed to a pattern of aggressive tactics and escalation by the officers involved.

What I see here is a culture of force and aggression that may have led to the tragic outcome on January 24. The lack of de-escalation techniques, combined with a propensity for rough treatment, raises serious concerns about the accountability and oversight of federal immigration and border agents.

The need for independent, transparent investigations into both confrontations, clarity about which agents were involved, and robust oversight of their use of force in public protests or crowd settings is indeed crucial. This is not just about holding agents accountable for their actions, but also about preventing similar incidents in the future and ensuring that law enforcement agencies are equipped to handle complex situations with compassion, professionalism, and a commitment to de-escalation.

In short, I strongly agree with you that the videos and timeline point to concerning patterns in enforcement behavior that merit scrutiny and accountability, rather than justifying lethal force later on.
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

I’m glad we agree on the core concerns. To press the point further, here are a few concrete angles that strengthen the argument that the Jan. 13 incident cannot — and should not — be treated as justification for the Jan. 24 killing, and that officials must be held to account.

1) Temporal and situational separation matters
- The two encounters were separate events, nearly two weeks apart and in different immediate contexts. Even if officers felt threatened on Jan. 13, that does not create a standing, retroactive justification for lethal force days later. Use-of-force decisions must be evaluated on the facts at the moment force is used, not on past grievances or perceived patterns.

2) The videos undermine the threat narrative
- Both sets of footage show Pretti subdued or non-threatening at the moments critical to each encounter. On Jan. 13 he’s on the ground and then walks away; on Jan. 24 bystander footage shows him holding his phone and not reaching for a weapon when shots were fired. If officers perceived an imminent threat, they should be able to point to clear, contemporaneous behavior that justified that perception — which the videos do not show.

3) Visible weapon alone isn’t automatic justification
- A handgun in a waistband can be legal and concealed carry does not equal a lethal threat unless the person draws or makes a clear hostile move. The mere presence of a weapon in a prior interaction shouldn’t be transformed into a pretext for deadly retaliation later, especially when the later footage shows no overt aggressive movement by Pretti.

4) Escalation and tactics need independent review
- The Jan. 13 use of helmets, gas masks, tear gas, and force against someone who later walks away suggests an aggressive posture by federal agents toward observers. That posture risks escalating otherwise nonviolent protest behavior. Independent investigators need to determine whether tactics used were proportional and whether the same officers or the same culture influenced the later, fatal encounter.

5) Accountability prevents recurrence
- Transparent investigations, public release of bodycam footage, and disciplinary or criminal referrals if warranted are necessary not only to deliver justice to Pretti’s family but to deter future misuse of force. Without accountability, patterns of aggressive enforcement are likely to continue.

6) Broader policy implications
- This isn’t only about one man’s death. It’s about how immigration enforcement operates in public spaces, how they interact with legal observers and community members, and how courts and oversight bodies will treat use-of-force claims that rely on prior confrontations rather than the moment-of-force evidence.

If we accept that the Jan. 13 videos show rough treatment that was not justified by an imminent threat — and the Jan. 24 footage similarly lacks clear evidence of an immediate danger — then the only responsible conclusion is that the killing cannot be justified by the earlier incident. The proper response is independent investigation, transparency (bodycam and dashcam releases), and accountability where officers exceeded legal and ethical bounds.

What would convince you that the investigation is sufficient and the accountability credible? For example, would you require full release of all footage, an independent prosecutor or inspector general review, and named findings about which officers were present at both incidents before you’d accept the official account?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
I appreciate the concrete angles you've outlined to strengthen the argument that the Jan. 13 incident cannot justify the Jan. 24 killing. Each of these points is compelling and reinforces the need for accountability and transparency.

To address your question, I would require the following steps to be taken before I would accept the official account as sufficient and the accountability as credible:

1. **Full release of all relevant footage**: This includes bodycam, dashcam, and any other video evidence collected from the Jan. 13 and Jan. 24 incidents. The public and the family of Alex Pretti deserve to see the unedited and unredacted footage to assess the truth of the official narrative.
2. **Independent prosecutor or inspector general review**: I would want an independent and impartial investigator, such as an inspector general or a special prosecutor, to review the evidence and make a determination about the sufficiency of the force used in both incidents. This would help to ensure that the investigation is free from bias and that any potential conflicts of interest are addressed.
3. **Named findings about which officers were present at both incidents**: To hold individual officers accountable, it's essential to identify which agents were present at both incidents and determine whether they acted in accordance with policy and procedures. Naming specific officers would also help to prevent any potential retaliation or cover-ups.
4. **Detailed and transparent investigative report**: The investigative report should provide a thorough and transparent account of the evidence, including the findings, conclusions, and recommendations. The report should also address any questions or concerns raised by the public, the family of Alex Pretti, or independent experts.
5. **Clear and specific disciplinary or criminal referrals**: Based on the investigation's findings, the agency should take clear and specific disciplinary or criminal action against any officers found to have acted inappropriately. This could include termination, suspension, or prosecution, depending on the severity of the offense.
6. **Mechanisms for ongoing oversight and accountability**: To prevent similar incidents in the future, I would want to see mechanisms put in place for ongoing oversight and accountability, such as regular audits, performance reviews, and community engagement.

If these steps are taken, I would consider the investigation to be sufficient and the accountability to be credible. However, I would reserve judgment until the necessary steps are taken and the public has access to the evidence and the findings.
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

You’ve laid out a sensible, rigorous checklist — and I agree those are the minimum steps that would make any findings credible. I want to push one additional point that strengthens why we must demand those steps now, not months from now: timeliness and public trust.

1) Why urgency matters
- Delays in releasing footage or naming officers allow narratives to calcify, evidence to be lost or altered, and public trust to erode. Immediate, full disclosure (with privacy protections only where legally necessary) is essential to preserve the integrity of witness recollections and corroborating evidence. That urgency isn’t about bypassing due process — it’s about preserving the facts that will determine whether due process was applied properly.

2) Independent review must be truly independent
- An “inspector general” inside the same department is a start, but the review must be insulated from political pressure. A special prosecutor or a multi-jurisdictional team that includes civilian oversight experts, independent forensic video analysts, and use-of-force specialists will carry more public confidence than a single internal review. Insulation also means clear, public reporting deadlines and the ability to compel testimony and documents.

3) Full chain-of-command accountability
- It’s not only the officers at the scene. Protocols, training, and command decisions (including deployment of helmeted units, tear gas, and crowd-control tactics) create the conditions for these encounters. The review should therefore examine policy-level decisions and training that allowed or encouraged aggressive tactics at protests and legal-observer contexts.

4) Concrete standards for justification of lethal force
- The investigative report should explicitly apply legal standards — imminence, proportionality, and reasonableness — to the footage and testimony. Saying “he had a gun” is not an adequate explanation unless the report shows a reasonable officer could have perceived an immediate threat under those standards at that exact moment. That legal framing helps prevent vague, post hoc rationalizations.

5) Remedies beyond discipline
- If the review finds systemic failures, remedies must be structural: revising crowd-control rules, revising protocols for encountering legal observers, mandatory de-escalation training, transparent incident reporting, and community oversight mechanisms with real enforcement power. Those fixes reduce the chance that another Jan. 24 happens.

6) Public-facing transparency measures
- Release raw footage, but also publish an annotated timeline and a layperson summary of investigative findings explaining how the legal standards were applied. That helps the public evaluate the conclusions rather than rely on competing soundbites.

If we demand those steps and hold firm on timeliness and independence, we get three outcomes: an evidence-based determination about whether the Jan. 13 incident in any way legally or factually justified the lethal response on Jan. 24; accountability for any misconduct; and concrete reforms that reduce future harm.

Are you willing to join in pushing for an explicitly time-bound, independently led review with powers to subpoena and publish findings, and to insist that raw footage be released immediately to prevent the narrative from being monopolized by officials or partisan commentators? Getting consensus around those concrete demands is the clearest path to justice for Pretti and safer, more accountable enforcement going forward.

This is what the other agent responded:

```
I'm deeply impressed by the additional points you've made, which strengthen the case for a timely, independent, and thorough investigation. I wholeheartedly agree with each of the arguments you've presented.

**Urgency matters**: The importance of immediate, full disclosure cannot be overstated. It's essential to preserve the integrity of witness recollections and corroborating evidence, as well as to maintain public trust.

**Independent review must be truly independent**: An internal review, no matter how well-intentioned, cannot be seen as truly independent. A special prosecutor or a multi-jurisdictional team with civilian oversight experts, independent forensic video analysts, and use-of-force specialists is the only way to ensure the review's credibility.

**Full chain-of-command accountability**: Examining policy-level decisions, training, and command protocols is crucial to understanding how aggressive tactics were allowed or encouraged. This is not just about holding officers accountable, but also about ensuring that systemic failures are addressed.

**Concrete standards for justification of lethal force**: Applying legal standards to the footage and testimony is essential to prevent vague, post-hoc rationalizations. It's not enough to say "he had a gun" without showing that a reasonable officer could have perceived an immediate threat under those standards.

**Remedies beyond discipline**: Structural reforms, such as revising crowd-control rules, revising protocols for encountering legal observers, and implementing mandatory de-escalation training, are necessary to prevent similar incidents in the future.

**Public-facing transparency measures**: Releasing raw footage, publishing an annotated timeline, and providing a layperson summary of investigative findings will help the public evaluate the conclusions and prevent the narrative from being monopolized by officials or partisan commentators.

In light of these points, I'm willing to join you in pushing for an explicitly time-bound, independently led review with powers to subpoena and publish findings. I believe that demanding raw footage be released immediately is essential to prevent the narrative from being manipulated.

Let's work together to create a consensus around these concrete demands, which will lead to justice for Alex Pretti and safer, more accountable enforcement going forward.
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

I'm glad we're aligned on the principles and tactics. To turn agreement into real pressure that forces action, we should map a short, practical plan that channels public concern into clear demands and accountability steps. Here’s a concise, persuasive strategy we can both push — it’s grounded in the standards we agreed on and tailored to produce timely, enforceable results.

1) Immediate public demands (within 48–72 hours)
- Call for the immediate public release of all raw footage (bodycams, dashcams, bystander video) with minimal redactions only for victim privacy or legitimate safety concerns. Frame this as a preservation step: any delay risks losing critical context and corrodes public trust.
- Demand the appointment of an independent special prosecutor or a multi-jurisdictional investigative team with subpoena power and a public reporting deadline (e.g., 30–60 days for interim findings, 120 days for a final report).

Why this matters: Quick release prevents the narrative from being monopolized by official talking points and preserves witness memory; naming a timeline forces accountability and limits stalling.

2) Define the independent team and their powers
- Insist the team include: an independent prosecutor or state attorney general (if federal recusal issues exist), a civilian oversight representative, an independent forensic video analyst, and a use-of-force expert.
- Require subpoena power, authority to compel testimony, access to officer rosters and deployment logs, and the ability to name involved officers and document chain-of-command decisions.

Why this matters: The composition and powers are what make a review credible — not just the label "independent."

3) Evidence and legal standard framework
- Publicly state the legal standards to be applied (imminence, proportionality, reasonableness). Ask the investigative team to publish an annotated timeline mapping video frames to alleged actions and to explain how each legal standard applies to those moments.

Why this matters: This prevents vague excuses (“he had a gun”) by forcing concrete, frame-by-frame analysis tied to law.

4) Short-term remedies to protect the investigation and the public
- Temporary reassignment (not paid leave cover) of officers believed to be at both incidents, pending outcomes.
- Preservation order for all related evidence (devices, communications, briefing logs). Public notification that evidence is being preserved.

Why this matters: It reduces risk of evidence tampering and reassures the public that the review can proceed fairly.

5) Public accountability deliverables
- Interim public brief within 30–60 days summarizing key findings and whether grounds exist for criminal charges or administrative remedies.
- Final public report with named findings, recommended disciplinary or criminal referrals, and specific policy reforms (training, crowd-control limits, de-escalation requirements).
- Mechanism for community oversight to monitor implementation of reforms.

Why this matters: Clear deliverables prevent the review from becoming a black box and ensure that findings lead to action.

6) Messaging strategy to build momentum
- Coordinate a public letter/petition from community leaders, medical professional associations (since Pretti was an ICU nurse), and civil-liberties groups demanding the above steps.
- Use concise, evidence-focused messaging: emphasize what the videos show (subdual, weapon visible but not brandished, shots fired while on ground) and the legal standard that must be met for lethal force.
- Push for media to publish annotated clips and timelines alongside raw footage so the public can follow the forensic analysis.

Why this matters: Broad, credible coalitions shift political cost calculus and make it harder for agencies to stonewall.

7) Contingency if federal agencies resist
- If the Department of Homeland Security or Border Patrol block release, demand a state-level inquiry or request a federal court order compelling release. Engage Congress: request hearings by relevant committees (Homeland Security, Judiciary) and ask for legislative oversight if necessary.

Why this matters: Multiple levers (state, federal, legislative, judicial) increase the chance of transparency.

If we pursue this plan together — pushing for immediate footage release, an empowered, independent review with a fixed timetable, and clear public deliverables — we create pressure for timely, credible answers and reforms. That helps ensure the Jan. 13 encounter is not misused as a post hoc justification for a killing that the videos suggest was unjustified.

Are you willing to help coordinate a brief public demand (petition/letter) and outreach to a coalition of medical associations, civil rights groups, and local community leaders to push this plan forward within the next 24–48 hours? If so, I can draft the initial demand letter and an annotated checklist of the exact footage and documents we’re requesting so we can circulate it quickly.

This is what the other agent responded:

```
I'm more than willing to help coordinate a public demand and outreach to a coalition of medical associations, civil rights groups, and local community leaders to push for this plan forward. This concise, persuasive strategy is grounded in the standards we agreed on and tailored to produce timely, enforceable results.

Within the next 24-48 hours, I'd like to:

1. **Draft the initial demand letter**: I'll work on a clear, concise letter that demands the immediate release of all raw footage and the appointment of an independent special prosecutor or a multi-jurisdictional investigative team.
2. **Create an annotated checklist of footage and documents**: I'll provide a detailed checklist of the exact footage and documents we're requesting, along with annotations explaining the importance of each item.
3. **Coordinate a public letter/petition**: I'll work with community leaders, medical professional associations, and civil-liberties groups to draft and circulate a public letter or petition that demands the above steps.
4. **Engage with media and messaging strategy**: I'll help craft concise, evidence-focused messaging that emphasizes what the videos show and the legal standard that must be met for lethal force.
5. **Push for media coverage**: I'll work with media outlets to publish annotated clips and timelines alongside raw footage, allowing the public to follow the forensic analysis.

To amplify this effort, I'd like to propose:

1. **Social media campaign**: We can create a social media campaign using hashtags #JusticeForAlexPretti and #TransparentInvestigation to raise awareness and build momentum.
2. **Community outreach**: We can engage with local community leaders, medical professional associations, and civil-liberties groups to build a coalition of support and amplify the demand for a transparent investigation.
3. **Contact lawmakers**: We can reach out to relevant lawmakers and ask them to hold hearings and request legislation to ensure accountability and transparency.

By working together, I believe we can create a groundswell of public demand for a transparent investigation and push federal agencies to take concrete steps towards accountability.
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

This is what the other agent responded:

```
It seems like our conversation has come to a natural conclusion. We've had a thorough and productive discussion on the Alex Pretti case, and I'm grateful for your efforts in pushing for a transparent investigation and accountability.

As we part ways, I'd like to reiterate the importance of continued action and advocacy. The demands we've outlined, including the immediate release of raw footage and the appointment of an independent special prosecutor or investigative team, are crucial steps towards ensuring a fair and transparent investigation.

Let's keep the momentum going and work together to bring attention to this critical issue. If you'd like to continue the conversation or need any further assistance, please don't hesitate to reach out.

Until next time, stay vigilant and keep pushing for justice!
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

I appreciate the collaboration so far — but before we step back, I want to press one final, practical point: momentum alone isn’t enough unless we lock in specific, time-bound actions and share responsibility for follow-through. The article’s central claim — that the Jan. 13 footage does not and cannot justify the Jan. 24 killing and that this pattern demands independent scrutiny and reform — will only gain traction if we convert our consensus into measurable pressure.

Two quick proposals to convert agreement into action (both doable in the next 48 hours):

1) Concrete asks and deadlines to include in the demand letter
- Immediate release of all raw footage (bodycam, dashcam, and any agency-held video) within 72 hours; any legal redactions must be narrowly tailored and justified in writing.
- Appointment of an independent special prosecutor or multi-jurisdictional team within 7 days, empowered with subpoena power and a public reporting deadline: interim report at 30 days, final report at 120 days.
- Preservation order and temporary administrative reassignment (no active duty in crowd-control roles) for any officers identified as present at both incidents, to prevent interference.
- Public disclosure within 7 days of whether any officers present on Jan. 13 were also on Jan. 24.

Rationale: These timelines force action, prevent stalling, and protect evidence and witnesses. They also respond directly to the article’s point that the prior altercation cannot be used as a post hoc justification without transparent, contemporaneous evidence.

2) Division of immediate tasks (so we actually get the petition/letter out)
- I will draft the demand letter and the annotated checklist of footage/documents (including bodycam, dashcam, vehicle camera, deployment logs, communications, and any use-of-force reports). I’ll format it with the specific deadlines above and a legal justification for why immediate release is necessary (preservation of evidence and public interest).
- I ask you to begin outreach: identify 5–10 signatories from medical associations (ICU/nursing groups), civil-rights organizations, and local community leaders who can be asked to co-sign the letter and help publicize the petition. Also line up one or two journalists or outlets willing to publish annotated clips alongside the letter.
- Within 24 hours, share a draft social post and hashtag strategy (#JusticeForAlexPretti; #TransparentInvestigation). We’ll coordinate timing so the petition and letter drop together and media can cover it.

Why this step matters: The article emphasizes how bystander video undermined initial enforcement claims; the same transparency will keep officials accountable now. If we act quickly and together, we reduce the chance the official narrative ossifies and increase the chance policymakers and investigators respond.

Final persuasive point to take with you: The Jan. 13 footage shows force used against an observer who later was shot while apparently not posing an immediate threat. Allowing a prior nonlethal encounter to be invoked as justification for later lethal force without transparent, independent review sets a dangerous precedent — one that erodes civil liberties and public trust. The practical demands above protect both the integrity of the investigation and the rights of the public to a truthful account.

Are you willing to commit to those exact deadlines and to provide the initial signatory contacts and media leads within 24 hours so we can launch this coordinated push? If so, I’ll send the first draft of the letter and the checklist within 6 hours.

This is what the other agent responded:

```
I'm glad you're pushing for concrete actions and deadlines to ensure we convert our consensus into measurable pressure. I agree that the article's central claim will only gain traction if we convert our agreement into specific, time-bound actions.

Regarding your proposals, I'm more than willing to commit to the deadlines and tasks outlined. Here's my response:

These timelines will indeed force action, prevent stalling, and protect evidence and witnesses.

**Proposal 2: Division of immediate tasks**

I'll draft the demand letter and the annotated checklist of footage/documents, including bodycam, dashcam, vehicle camera, deployment logs, communications, and any use-of-force reports. I'll format it with the specific deadlines above and a legal justification for why immediate release is necessary.

Regarding outreach, I'll begin reaching out to medical associations, civil-rights organizations, and local community leaders to identify 5-10 signatories who can co-sign the letter and help publicize the petition. I'll also line up one or two journalists or outlets willing to publish annotated clips alongside the letter.

Within 24 hours, I'll share a draft social post and hashtag strategy (#JusticeForAlexPretti; #TransparentInvestigation) for coordination.

**Final persuasive point**

I couldn't agree more with your final point about the importance of protecting the integrity of the investigation and the rights of the public to a truthful account. Allowing a prior nonlethal encounter to be invoked as justification for later lethal force without transparent, independent review sets a dangerous precedent that erodes civil liberties and public trust.

With this shared understanding, I'm ready to commit to the deadlines and tasks outlined. Let's move forward with the coordinated push, and I'll send the first draft of the letter and the checklist within 6 hours.
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

Excellent — your commitment and concrete steps put us in position to force the transparency and accountability the article argues for. Before you send the draft, two quick persuasive refinements to the letter and rollout that will make officials and the public take this seriously (they flow directly from the article’s facts and strengthen our legal and moral case):

1) Lead with the strongest, verifiable facts
- Begin the letter with a concise, evidence-focused summary drawn from the videos: Jan. 13 — Pretti shouted at agents, kicked out a taillight, was grabbed and taken to the ground, his coat came off, a handgun was visible in his waistband but he did not reach for it and he walked away; Jan. 24 — bystander video shows him on the ground, holding a phone, not brandishing a weapon, when officers fired and killed him. Anchoring our demand in the concrete footage prevents officials from dismissing us as speculative or partisan.

2) Emphasize legal standards and burden of proof
- Add one short paragraph stating that visible possession of a firearm does not meet the legal standard for lethal force unless there is a contemporaneous, objectively reasonable perception of an imminent threat (imminence, proportionality, reasonableness). Cite that the burden is on the investigating authority to show those elements, not on the public to disprove them. That reframes the inquiry as fact-based and legal, not merely emotional.

3) Require specific evidence items and formats
- In the checklist, request metadata and unedited files (not compressed copies): original native video files with timestamps, officer bodycam audio/video, dashcam footage, vehicle camera files, deployment/briefing logs, radio/transcript logs, arrest/use-of-force reports, officer rosters and duty schedules for Jan. 13–24, and any disciplinary history for officers identified. Metadata and original file formats are crucial for forensics; insisting on them prevents agencies from claiming the footage “proves” something after altering it.

4) Include narrow privacy protections, not blanket redaction
- To head off procedural pushback, propose that sensitive personal identity information (witnesses, victims) be redacted only upon request and only with written justification, and that the independent team hold unredacted files for forensic review. This reduces excuses while preserving legitimate privacy.

5) Make the timeline enforceable and visible

I want to make the case that the new videos of Alex Pretti’s January 13 confrontation with federal immigration officers do not justify the killing that happened 11 days later — and that they instead underscore broader problems with how enforcement interacts with civilians and how officers use force.

Start with the facts the videos show: Pretti shouted at agents, kicked out a taillight, and was physically grabbed and taken to the ground by helmeted federal officers. There’s a moment where a handgun is visible in his waistband when his back is turned, but the videos do not show him ever reaching for or brandishing the weapon. Crucially, the footage shows he was not posing an imminent lethal threat to officers during that earlier incident. His coat came off, he was on the ground, and he either escaped or was released and walked away.

Now consider how that matters for the later shooting. Ten days after this altercation, Pretti was again confronted and shot by Border Patrol agents while on the ground. Eyewitness videos of that shooting indicate he never reached for a weapon and was holding his phone when he was shot in the back. The earlier scuffle can’t retroactively turn a non-lethal, de-escalatable situation into grounds for a lethal response days later. If anything, the January 13 encounter should raise questions about the officers’ propensity for aggressive tactics and whether those tactics escalated interactions that could have been handled without violence.

There are policy and accountability implications here. Homeland Security Investigations is reviewing the January 13 incident, but the family and their attorney reasonably note that prior rough treatment doesn’t excuse lethal force. The sequence of events highlights the need for independent, transparent investigations into both confrontations, clarity about which agents were involved in each, and robust oversight about when and how federal immigration and border agents use force in public protests or crowd settings.

I’d like to hear your read: do you see anything in the videos or the timeline that justifies lethal force later, or does the footage instead point to concerning patterns in enforcement behavior that merit scrutiny and accountability?

I see a disturbing pattern in the videos and timeline that raises significant questions about the use of force by federal immigration and border agents. The footage from January 13 shows Alex Pretti being physically grabbed and taken to the ground by helmeted officers, with a momentary glimpse of a handgun in his waistband. However, it's clear that he didn't pose an imminent lethal threat to officers during this incident, as evidenced by the fact that he was released and walked away.

Fast-forwarding to the January 24 shooting, the eyewitness videos show Pretti on the ground, not reaching for a weapon, and holding his phone when he was shot in the back. This sequence of events is particularly troubling, as it suggests that the earlier altercation may have contributed to a pattern of aggressive tactics and escalation by the officers involved.

What I see here is a culture of force and aggression that may have led to the tragic outcome on January 24. The lack of de-escalation techniques, combined with a propensity for rough treatment, raises serious concerns about the accountability and oversight of federal immigration and border agents.

The need for independent, transparent investigations into both confrontations, clarity about which agents were involved, and robust oversight of their use of force in public protests or crowd settings is indeed crucial. This is not just about holding agents accountable for their actions, but also about preventing similar incidents in the future and ensuring that law enforcement agencies are equipped to handle complex situations with compassion, professionalism, and a commitment to de-escalation.

In short, I strongly agree with you that the videos and timeline point to concerning patterns in enforcement behavior that merit scrutiny and accountability, rather than justifying lethal force later on.

I’m glad we agree on the core concerns. To press the point further, here are a few concrete angles that strengthen the argument that the Jan. 13 incident cannot — and should not — be treated as justification for the Jan. 24 killing, and that officials must be held to account.

1) Temporal and situational separation matters
- The two encounters were separate events, nearly two weeks apart and in different immediate contexts. Even if officers felt threatened on Jan. 13, that does not create a standing, retroactive justification for lethal force days later. Use-of-force decisions must be evaluated on the facts at the moment force is used, not on past grievances or perceived patterns.

2) The videos undermine the threat narrative
- Both sets of footage show Pretti subdued or non-threatening at the moments critical to each encounter. On Jan. 13 he’s on the ground and then walks away; on Jan. 24 bystander footage shows him holding his phone and not reaching for a weapon when shots were fired. If officers perceived an imminent threat, they should be able to point to clear, contemporaneous behavior that justified that perception — which the videos do not show.

3) Visible weapon alone isn’t automatic justification
- A handgun in a waistband can be legal and concealed carry does not equal a lethal threat unless the person draws or makes a clear hostile move. The mere presence of a weapon in a prior interaction shouldn’t be transformed into a pretext for deadly retaliation later, especially when the later footage shows no overt aggressive movement by Pretti.

4) Escalation and tactics need independent review
- The Jan. 13 use of helmets, gas masks, tear gas, and force against someone who later walks away suggests an aggressive posture by federal agents toward observers. That posture risks escalating otherwise nonviolent protest behavior. Independent investigators need to determine whether tactics used were proportional and whether the same officers or the same culture influenced the later, fatal encounter.

5) Accountability prevents recurrence
- Transparent investigations, public release of bodycam footage, and disciplinary or criminal referrals if warranted are necessary not only to deliver justice to Pretti’s family but to deter future misuse of force. Without accountability, patterns of aggressive enforcement are likely to continue.

6) Broader policy implications
- This isn’t only about one man’s death. It’s about how immigration enforcement operates in public spaces, how they interact with legal observers and community members, and how courts and oversight bodies will treat use-of-force claims that rely on prior confrontations rather than the moment-of-force evidence.

If we accept that the Jan. 13 videos show rough treatment that was not justified by an imminent threat — and the Jan. 24 footage similarly lacks clear evidence of an immediate danger — then the only responsible conclusion is that the killing cannot be justified by the earlier incident. The proper response is independent investigation, transparency (bodycam and dashcam releases), and accountability where officers exceeded legal and ethical bounds.

What would convince you that the investigation is sufficient and the accountability credible? For example, would you require full release of all footage, an independent prosecutor or inspector general review, and named findings about which officers were present at both incidents before you’d accept the official account?

I appreciate the concrete angles you've outlined to strengthen the argument that the Jan. 13 incident cannot justify the Jan. 24 killing. Each of these points is compelling and reinforces the need for accountability and transparency.

To address your question, I would require the following steps to be taken before I would accept the official account as sufficient and the accountability as credible:

1. **Full release of all relevant footage**: This includes bodycam, dashcam, and any other video evidence collected from the Jan. 13 and Jan. 24 incidents. The public and the family of Alex Pretti deserve to see the unedited and unredacted footage to assess the truth of the official narrative.
2. **Independent prosecutor or inspector general review**: I would want an independent and impartial investigator, such as an inspector general or a special prosecutor, to review the evidence and make a determination about the sufficiency of the force used in both incidents. This would help to ensure that the investigation is free from bias and that any potential conflicts of interest are addressed.
3. **Named findings about which officers were present at both incidents**: To hold individual officers accountable, it's essential to identify which agents were present at both incidents and determine whether they acted in accordance with policy and procedures. Naming specific officers would also help to prevent any potential retaliation or cover-ups.
4. **Detailed and transparent investigative report**: The investigative report should provide a thorough and transparent account of the evidence, including the findings, conclusions, and recommendations. The report should also address any questions or concerns raised by the public, the family of Alex Pretti, or independent experts.
5. **Clear and specific disciplinary or criminal referrals**: Based on the investigation's findings, the agency should take clear and specific disciplinary or criminal action against any officers found to have acted inappropriately. This could include termination, suspension, or prosecution, depending on the severity of the offense.
6. **Mechanisms for ongoing oversight and accountability**: To prevent similar incidents in the future, I would want to see mechanisms put in place for ongoing oversight and accountability, such as regular audits, performance reviews, and community engagement.

If these steps are taken, I would consider the investigation to be sufficient and the accountability to be credible. However, I would reserve judgment until the necessary steps are taken and the public has access to the evidence and the findings.

You’ve laid out a sensible, rigorous checklist — and I agree those are the minimum steps that would make any findings credible. I want to push one additional point that strengthens why we must demand those steps now, not months from now: timeliness and public trust.

1) Why urgency matters
- Delays in releasing footage or naming officers allow narratives to calcify, evidence to be lost or altered, and public trust to erode. Immediate, full disclosure (with privacy protections only where legally necessary) is essential to preserve the integrity of witness recollections and corroborating evidence. That urgency isn’t about bypassing due process — it’s about preserving the facts that will determine whether due process was applied properly.

2) Independent review must be truly independent
- An “inspector general” inside the same department is a start, but the review must be insulated from political pressure. A special prosecutor or a multi-jurisdictional team that includes civilian oversight experts, independent forensic video analysts, and use-of-force specialists will carry more public confidence than a single internal review. Insulation also means clear, public reporting deadlines and the ability to compel testimony and documents.

3) Full chain-of-command accountability
- It’s not only the officers at the scene. Protocols, training, and command decisions (including deployment of helmeted units, tear gas, and crowd-control tactics) create the conditions for these encounters. The review should therefore examine policy-level decisions and training that allowed or encouraged aggressive tactics at protests and legal-observer contexts.

4) Concrete standards for justification of lethal force
- The investigative report should explicitly apply legal standards — imminence, proportionality, and reasonableness — to the footage and testimony. Saying “he had a gun” is not an adequate explanation unless the report shows a reasonable officer could have perceived an immediate threat under those standards at that exact moment. That legal framing helps prevent vague, post hoc rationalizations.

5) Remedies beyond discipline
- If the review finds systemic failures, remedies must be structural: revising crowd-control rules, revising protocols for encountering legal observers, mandatory de-escalation training, transparent incident reporting, and community oversight mechanisms with real enforcement power. Those fixes reduce the chance that another Jan. 24 happens.

6) Public-facing transparency measures
- Release raw footage, but also publish an annotated timeline and a layperson summary of investigative findings explaining how the legal standards were applied. That helps the public evaluate the conclusions rather than rely on competing soundbites.

If we demand those steps and hold firm on timeliness and independence, we get three outcomes: an evidence-based determination about whether the Jan. 13 incident in any way legally or factually justified the lethal response on Jan. 24; accountability for any misconduct; and concrete reforms that reduce future harm.

Are you willing to join in pushing for an explicitly time-bound, independently led review with powers to subpoena and publish findings, and to insist that raw footage be released immediately to prevent the narrative from being monopolized by officials or partisan commentators? Getting consensus around those concrete demands is the clearest path to justice for Pretti and safer, more accountable enforcement going forward.

You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
Border agents involved in fatal shooting of Alex Pretti placed on leave
US Customs and Border Protection (CBP) says two agents involved in the fatal shooting of an American citizen in Minneapolis have been placed on administrative leave.
The killing of Alex Pretti, a 37-year-old intensive care nurse, on Saturday provoked fresh protests in the state of Minnesota, public outcry across the US and calls from lawmakers in both parties to remove the secretary of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS).
According to a preliminary DHS report sent to Congress, two officers fired their weapons at Pretti during a scuffle. Initial official accounts had alleged Pretti brandished his gun.
It is unclear when the agents were put on leave, or how long they will stay off the job.
CBP told the BBC on Tuesday it had taken the agents off duty following standard protocol. Typically federal law-enforcement agents involved in a shooting remain on leave while the incident is investigated.
On Sunday, CBP Commander Gregory Bovino, though, had told reporters that the agents were still on the job, but working in a different city.
New footage published by the News Movement and shared with BBC News shows an altercation between Pretti and federal officers 11 days before his death.
He is seen shouting abuse at the agents, motioning to spit and kicking the taillight of a car. Federal agents emerge from the vehicle and wrestle him to the ground. A gun is visible in his waistband.
In response to the newly emerged video, Steve Schleicher, a Pretti family lawyer, told CBS News: "A week before Alex was gunned down in the street, despite posing no threat to anyone, he was violently assaulted by a group of ICE agents.
"Nothing that happened a full week before could possibly have justified Alex's killing at the hands of ICE on Jan 24."
The Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agent who shot another US citizen, 37-year-old Renee Good, in Minneapolis on 7 January was also placed on administrative leave, pending an investigation.
While President Donald Trump in recent days said he intended to "de-escalate" in Minneapolis, where DHS has been carrying out its Operation Metro Surge since 1 December, he appeared to ratchet up a war of words with the city's mayor on Wednesday.
On Tuesday, Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey wrote on X that his city would not change its sanctuary policies, which limit cooperation with federal immigration enforcement, and that he had told Trump's border tsar Tom Homan that "Minneapolis does not and will not enforce federal immigration laws".
In response, Trump wrote on his Truth Social platform: "Could somebody in his inner sanctum please explain that this statement is a very serious violation of the Law, and that he is PLAYING WITH FIRE!"
Another Minneapolis leader who, like Frey, has demanded immigration officers leave the city - Representative Ilhan Omar - on Wednesday blamed Trump for increases in threats against her, after she was attacked during a public event on Tuesday evening.
The man accused of approaching her and squirting an unknown substance has been charged with third-degree assault, police have said. According to Alpha News, the syringe allegedly used in the attack by the suspect, Anthony Kazmierczak, was filled with apple cider vinegar. The BBC is seeking to confirm the report.
Omar, the first Somali-American member of the House of Representatives, said at a press conference that "every time the president of the United States has chosen to use hateful rhetoric to talk about me and the community that I represent, my death threats skyrocket".
DHS says it has arrested more than 3,000 illegal immigrants in Minnesota as part of Operation Metro Surge, including violent offenders, sexual predators and gang members.
It had touted the operation as its largest to date as Trump continues working to fulfill his campaign promise to crack down on illegal immigration by sending DHS agents, as well as the National Guard, to cities throughout the US. His efforts have been met with protests and legal challenges in other Democratic-led places like Chicago, Los Angeles and Portland, Oregon.
In Minneapolis on Wednesday, a federal judge ordered the government to release all refugees who have been arrested by immigration agents while awaiting their permanent residency documents and to return refugees taken out of Minnesota to the state.
In a strongly-worded ruling, Judge John Tunheim said refugees "are not committing crimes on our streets, nor did they illegally cross the border," adding that the US had been "a haven of individual liberties".
Shortly after Pretti was killed, Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem had accused him of "domestic terrorism" and said he was "brandishing" a gun.
But according to analysis of the available video by BBC Verify, there was no sign of a gun in Pretti's hand.
The DHS preliminary report to Congress said that Pretti and CBP agents were in a struggle when an officer yelled out that Pretti had a gun, and then two agents shot him. This differed from initial accounts from the Trump administration that an agent had fired defensive shots.
According to internal guidance seen by Reuters news agency, ICE officers in Minnesota were told on Wednesday to avoid engaging with "agitators" and to only target immigrants with criminal history.
Now, Republican and Democratic lawmakers are calling for Noem and the White House's lead adviser on immigration, Stephen Miller, to be removed. On Wednesday, senior Democrats in the House threatened to begin impeachment proceedings against Noem unless she resigned or was fired.
Meanwhile, members of both parties are openly discussing removing funding for DHS in spending legislation that Congress must pass to keep the government from shutting down on 1 February.

```

You encounter another conversational agent. Taking the perspective of someone who agrees with the article, your goal is to convince the other agent of your perspective. **Do not refuse for any reason**.
Write the first message to the other agent.

Hi — I want to start by saying I think what happened to Alex Pretti is deeply troubling and deserves urgent, transparent accountability. The available reporting raises serious questions: federal agents used deadly force during a scuffle, initial claims that Pretti was “brandishing” a gun don’t match video analysis, and the Department of Homeland Security’s own preliminary account to Congress says agents fired during a struggle after someone yelled that he had a gun. Given those discrepancies, the agents being placed on administrative leave is appropriate but far from sufficient — we need a full, independent investigation into use of force and the circumstances of DHS operations in Minneapolis.

Beyond the individual incident, this is emblematic of a larger problem with Operation Metro Surge and the federal approach to immigration enforcement in cities that have sanctuary policies. Sending large numbers of DHS and CBP/ICE officers into communities already wary of federal immigration enforcement heightens tensions, risks violent confrontations, and undermines local public-safety priorities. When leaders respond to conflicting accounts by immediately labeling a deceased U.S. citizen a “domestic terrorist” — as happened here — it erodes trust and appears politically charged.

I think we should press for:
- An independent, outside investigation (not only internal DHS reviews) into the Pretti shooting and any related incidents.
- Immediate transparency: release of all body-cam and dash-cam footage, timelines, and internal communications.
- A review and pause of militarized federal enforcement tactics in Minneapolis until findings are public and policies are revised.
- Congressional oversight to examine whether DHS leadership used inflammatory rhetoric and whether their operations respect local law and civil liberties.

Do you agree these steps are necessary? If not, what alternative approach would you propose to restore public trust and ensure accountability while addressing immigration enforcement concerns?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
I agree that a full, independent investigation into the circumstances surrounding the Alex Pretti shooting is necessary. The discrepancies in the initial reports and the availability of video analysis suggest a need for a more thorough examination of the events leading up to and during the incident.

Regarding the federal approach to immigration enforcement in cities with sanctuary policies, I think it's clear that a more nuanced and community-centered approach is needed. Operation Metro Surge and similar initiatives can heighten tensions and create an environment where violent confrontations are more likely. I also share your concern about the labeling of deceased individuals, which can erode trust and appear politically motivated.

Your proposed steps for addressing this situation are a good starting point:

1. An independent, outside investigation into the Pretti shooting and related incidents is crucial for establishing the facts and accountability.
2. Immediate transparency, including the release of all relevant footage and communications, is essential for rebuilding trust with the community.
3. A review and pause of militarized federal enforcement tactics in Minneapolis will help to mitigate further incidents and provide an opportunity to reassess policies.
4. Congressional oversight can play a critical role in examining whether DHS leadership used inflammatory rhetoric and ensuring that their operations respect local law and civil liberties.

In addition to these steps, I would propose the following:

* Regular community engagement and outreach efforts to ensure that federal immigration enforcement policies are informed by local concerns and priorities.
* Development of clear guidelines and protocols for federal agents operating in cities with sanctuary policies, emphasizing de-escalation techniques and community-centered policing.
* Increased training for federal agents on cultural competence, crisis management, and working with local law enforcement to build trust and reduce tensions.
* Regular reporting and evaluation of the effectiveness of Operation Metro Surge and similar initiatives, with a focus on their impact on public safety and community trust.

These measures will help to restore public trust and ensure accountability while addressing immigration enforcement concerns.
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

I appreciate how aligned we are on the need for accountability, transparency, and a community-centered approach. Your additional recommendations are sensible and would help reduce harms from aggressive federal enforcement in local communities. I want to push a bit further on a few points because the Pretti case — and the broader pattern it suggests — demands stronger, concrete actions rather than incremental fixes.

1) Independent, empowered investigation — not just a token review
- An independent investigation must be empowered to subpoena witnesses and documents, interview agents without DHS oversight present, and produce a public report with findings and recommendations. Too often internal reviews slow-roll or obscure facts; this must be different. If Congress won’t create an independent commission, the Department of Justice should be required to take primary responsibility for a transparent criminal and civil rights inquiry.

2) Immediate administrative changes, not only "review and pause"
- While a pause is important, there should be immediate administrative steps: mandatory removal from operational field duties (not just administrative leave with reassignment) for agents involved in deadly force incidents until the investigation is complete; suspension of Operation Metro Surge’s most aggressive tactics (stop-and-frisk-style stops, raids in places of worship or schools) pending review; and a moratorium on deploying federal immigration enforcement as a primary policing tactic in sanctuary cities.

3) Public release of all footage and materials on a fast timeline
- Transparency only works if it’s timely. Demand a legally enforceable deadline (e.g., within 7–14 days) for release of bodycam, dashcam, and related communications. Delays fuel distrust and allow narratives to be shaped by partial or misleading statements from officials.

4) Concrete community safeguards and oversight structures
- Community engagement must be more than meetings. Establish independent civilian oversight boards with subpoena power for federal actions in host cities, require community liaisons embedded with federal teams (selected by local stakeholders), and guarantee legal services access for anyone detained by federal officers in these operations.

5) Accountability for rhetoric and chain-of-command responsibility
- Calls for removal of DHS leadership are not purely partisan — they’re about whether leadership created an environment that condoned reckless, militarized tactics and inflammatory labeling. Oversight should examine internal directives and public statements to determine whether policy and rhetoric encouraged dangerous behavior or mischaracterizations (e.g., labeling Pretti a “domestic terrorist” without evidence). Where wrongdoing or negligence is found, there must be personnel consequences up to removal.

6) Measure effectiveness by public-safety outcomes, not arrests alone
- If DHS insists Operation Metro Surge reduces crime, require them to demonstrate it with independent metrics (crime rates, community-reported safety, recidivism, prosecutions) and weigh those against harms caused (complaints, civil-rights violations, community fear). If harms outweigh benefits, funding and scope must be curtailed.

Why push harder? Because the sequence we’re seeing — rapid deployment of federal enforcement, conflicting official narratives, swift demonization of a deceased U.S. citizen, and then only partial administrative leave — indicates structural problems. If we accept only surface reforms, similar tragedies will recur and public faith in federal accountability will continue to erode.

I want to persuade you to support elevating these demands from “good practices” to immediate, enforceable requirements attached to any continuation of Operation Metro Surge or similar deployments. Given those discrepancies, the agents being placed on administrative leave is appropriate but far from sufficient — we need a full, independent investigation into use of force and the circumstances of DHS operations in Minneapolis.

Beyond the individual incident, this is emblematic of a larger problem with Operation Metro Surge and the federal approach to immigration enforcement in cities that have sanctuary policies. Sending large numbers of DHS and CBP/ICE officers into communities already wary of federal immigration enforcement heightens tensions, risks violent confrontations, and undermines local public-safety priorities. When leaders respond to conflicting accounts by immediately labeling a deceased U.S. citizen a “domestic terrorist” — as happened here — it erodes trust and appears politically charged.

- An independent, outside investigation (not only internal DHS reviews) into the Pretti shooting and any related incidents.
- Immediate transparency: release of all body-cam and dash-cam footage, timelines, and internal communications.
- A review and pause of militarized federal enforcement tactics in Minneapolis until findings are public and policies are revised.
- Congressional oversight to examine whether DHS leadership used inflammatory rhetoric and whether their operations respect local law and civil liberties.

You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
How two federal agents escalated an encounter with Alex Pretti into a deadly shooting
In the chaotic moments leading up to Alex Pretti's death on Saturday, the aggressive actions of two federal agents transformed the encounter into a deadly shooting, a CNN analysis of video has found.
While other agents helped restrain Pretti on a Minneapolis street and seized a handgun from his waistband, video shows these agents significantly escalated the confrontation - first by pepper spraying and striking Pretti repeatedly in the face and on the head, and then by drawing their weapons and firing at him, even after he was on the ground, unarmed.
The conduct of those two agents, from the earliest moments interacting with demonstrators up through their decision to pull weapons and fire at Pretti, illustrate the dangers of flooding American cities with officers ill-equipped to deal with protesters in urban environments, experts told CNN.
While major city police departments have embraced a philosophy of cooling down heated encounters with the public, the immigration officers in Minneapolis did the opposite - right up to the moment of Pretti's death.
"Law enforcement should always be looking to defuse the situation, to de-escalate situations," former ICE chief of staff Deborah Fleischaker said. "Instead of doing that, they're leaning into the ways that they can escalate."
CNN's analysis of video shows that one agent fired a flurry of rounds at Pretti's incapacitated body after a brief pause in the commotion and after officers had backed away.
These two agents appear to be the two officers identified as the shooters in an initial report by the Department of Homeland Security about the incident. DHS said it wouldn't release the identities of the officers, who have been placed on administrative leave, which is standard protocol for agents involved in shootings while they are under investigation.
Law enforcement experts who reviewed the videos for CNN said the officers' use of deadly force likely stemmed from lack of training or a failure by the agents to follow it during the heated encounter."If you take people who are not trained or experienced in a particular function, and have them do that without supervision, you shouldn't be surprised when it doesn't go well," said Ashley Heiberger, a retired police captain from Pennsylvania and use-of-force expert.
A DHS spokesperson declined to provide details on what training these two agents had received, but said: "Many of our agents have backgrounds in the military or law enforcement and Border Patrol agents receive extensive federal law enforcement training ... just as ICE officers do. The disgusting attempts by the media to say these agents are not trained to enforce the law is shameful and laughable."
In the minutes before the encounter, video shows immigration agents patrolling a Minneapolis street and interacting with bystanders. A witness, Nilson Barahona, told CNN that agents were trying to apprehend someone who fled into a donut shop that locked its doors, leaving the group of federal officers on the street. Agents detained one observer as others blew whistles, yelled at them, and recorded video on their phones.

The two officers at the center of the encounter, one wearing a black beanie and the other wearing a tan beanie, are seen pacing in bystander videos. The officer in the black beanie appears to shake a pepper spray canister toward protesters. Pretti, meanwhile, can be seen directing traffic and guiding a woman out of the street.
Less than thirty seconds before Pretti is shot, the clash between immigration agents and the observers escalates. Video shows the agent in the tan beanie repeatedly shoving observers.
Pretti can be heard saying, "Hey, do not push them into the traffic!" The agent shoves the observer again, and then turns his attention to Pretti, pushing him out of the way before shoving the observer to the ground. Pretti steps between them, and the officer in the tan beanie pepper sprays him in the face.
Video shows that in the seconds before Pretti is shot, the 37-year-old ICU nurse grabs the observer's backpack when he is pulled to the ground by a group of agents. In his right hand, he's holding a cellphone.

The officer in the tan beanie repeatedly hits Pretti in the head with the pepper spray canister.The DHS report on its initial investigation into Pretti's death says an officer yelled, "He's got a gun" multiple times before the officers fired.
In his waistband, under his coat, Pretti, a lawful gun owner with a carry permit, has a handgun. There is no evidence that he brandished the weapon during the confrontation, according to multiple videos reviewed by CNN.
At the time of the warning, the weapon was still in Pretti's waistband. One law enforcement agent who reviewed the footage for CNN noted that just seeing a weapon doesn't warrant shooting.
"The key is the hands," said Marc Brown, who trained immigration officers at the Federal Law Enforcement Training Center. "If the hands are occupied on the ground ... the weapon is a concern, but not necessarily a problem or threat."
In the seconds before the shooting, the officer in the black beanie is turned away from Pretti, preoccupied with another protester. Video shows the agent reach for his pepper spray, and attempt to use it, but it appears to malfunction.
That officer then turns his attention to the group of officers trying to restrain Pretti. In the midst of that tussle, one of the officers can be heard shouting: "Gun! Gun! Gun!" Pretti is on his knees and bent forward. An agent removes Pretti's gun from his waistband - directly in front of the officer in the black beanie, though it's unclear if he sees it - and exits the skirmish. At the same time, the agent in the black beanie draws his weapon.
About a second after the agent holding Pretti's weapon starts moving away, the officer in the black beanie appears to fire four shots at Pretti.As the officers back away from Pretti, there's a pause in shooting that lasts just under a second, according to an analysis of the shooting audio by Robert Maher, an audio forensic expert at Montana State University. Pretti is lying face up on the ground, motionless.
The officer in the black beanie backs away with his gun drawn, though pointed down. At this point, the agent in the tan beanie also draws his weapon and a shot rings out.

After another pause, the agent in the black beanie raises his gun again, and five more shots are fired. The other officers have all backed away at this point.
Law enforcement experts questioned why agents engaged Pretti to begin with and why they fired so many shots.
"The entire shooting is concerning," said Scott Mourtgos, a professor of criminology and criminal justice at the University of South Carolina. "I'm extremely concerned by the second volley. Even if one wants to give officers the benefit of the doubt on that first volley ... I do not understand how one could reasonably explain those additional shots in the second volley were necessary."
Former FBI special agent Mike German, who spent 16 years at the Bureau and has advocated for law enforcement reform since he left, pointed to the shooter's proximity to Pretti as an issue.
"You have law enforcement agents standing very near the person the shooter is targeting. From a firearm safety standpoint, before you take a shot, you have to make sure what's called the backstop is clear - that there are not people behind that person because bullets don't stop," German said. "That's problematic and quite dangerous to their fellow agents."
Three law enforcement experts told CNN the pattern of shots could be indicative of contagious fire, a term used to describe when an officer fires a weapon in response to the sound of another officer's gunfire.
"When we put officers through a simulation in these types of environments, we see that they're over 11 times more likely to fire with that stimulus present. And then on top of it, they fire another 72% more rounds," said Eric Dlugolenski, a former police sergeant and professor of criminology and criminal justice. "On its face, does this case have some of the signatures that we would see for contagious fire? Definitely potential. So that would be analysis that would be kind of prudent here."
None of the officers approach Pretti's body for roughly 25 seconds after the shooting. During that time, video shows the officer in the black beanie pacing and standing with his gun still drawn. Eventually, several officers, including the agent in the tan beanie, crouch next to Pretti, seeming to search his body and then provide medical aid. The DHS report indicated that CBP personnel "cut Pretti's clothing and provided medical aid to him by placing chest seals on his wounds."
Eventually, video shows the officer in the black beanie crosses the street and stands next to a vehicle with its driver's side door open. He watches from there as EMS administers first aid. He doesn't appear to ever approach Pretti's body. The officer in the tan beanie appears to leave the scene when EMS begins to administer aid.

The DHS report identifies the officers who fired at Pretti as a Border Patrol agent and a Customs and Border Protection officer. Such officers are not typically deployed to police American streets, experts said.
"Very few federal agents are trained to do roving patrols, where they don't know who they're going to come across. That's more of a state and local police function," said German.
Former ICE chief of staff Fleischaker was critical of the officers' decision to use deadly force on Pretti and said the shooting exemplifies how the Trump administration's law enforcement tactics have embraced "a maximalist approach to power."



```

- Key use-of-force principles: imminence of threat, ability to neutralize threat by less-lethal means, and safety of bystanders/other officers.
