user_messages.content:
You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
Alex Pretti, 37, identified as man fatally shot by Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis
AFGE says video doesn't clearly show Alex Jeffrey Pretti brandishing weapon as DHS claimed in Minneapolis shooting
The American Federation of Government Employees (AFGE) identified the man fatally shot by a Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis Saturday as one of its members, Alex Jeffrey Pretti.
Pretti, a 37-year-old Minneapolis resident and U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs ICU nurse, was shot during an immigration enforcement operation early Saturday targeting Jose Huerta-Chuma, an illegal immigrant with a criminal history including domestic assault for intentional conflict bodily harm, disorderly conduct and driving without a valid license.
Homeland Security officials said Pretti approached Border Patrol agents while armed with a 9 mm pistol and "violently resisted" when they attempted to disarm him.
Medics immediately delivered aid, but Pretti was pronounced dead at the site of the shooting.
State officials said Pretti had a lawful permit to carry (PTC).
Court records indicate his two-year marriage was dissolved in 2023 without children, and he did not have a criminal record, aside from minor traffic violations.
The Department of Homeland Security is leading the investigation into the shooting, with assistance from the FBI.
Pretti's father, Michael, told The Associated Press his son, a University of Minnesota graduate, started getting involved in protests after the fatal Jan. 7 shooting of Renee Nicole Good by an ICE agent, noting he was "very upset with what was happening in Minneapolis and throughout the United States with ICE."
"He thought it was terrible, you know, kidnapping children, just grabbing people off the street," Michael Pretti said. "He cared about those people, and he knew it was wrong, so he did participate in protests."
His parents said they recently told him to be careful when protesting, asking him not to "engage" or "do anything stupid."
"He said he knows that. He knew that," Michael Pretti told the outlet.
The family said they did not know about the shooting until they were contacted by a reporter and couldn't get any information from Border Patrol or local hospitals.
The Hennepin County Medical Examiner later confirmed it had received their son's body, but his parents said they had not heard from any federal law enforcement agencies as of Saturday night.
"The sickening lies told about our son by the administration are reprehensible and disgusting," the family wrote in a statement obtained by the AP. "Alex is clearly not holding a gun when attacked by Trump's murdering and cowardly ICE thugs. He has his phone in his right hand and his empty left hand is raised above his head while trying to protect the woman ICE just pushed down all while being pepper sprayed. Please get the truth out about our son. He was a good man. Thank you."
The AFGE said, as of Saturday night, "many of the details remain unclear."
"Video of the incident is circulating on social media, and the Department of Homeland Security has publicly stated that the victim was brandishing a weapon at officers. However, based on the video currently available, that claim is not clearly established," the AFGE wrote. 
"This is an unfolding and extremely serious matter. Until we have verified facts, it is important that we refrain from speculation or drawing conclusions.
"What we do know is this: a member of our union lost their life today, and that alone is devastating. Our hearts are heavy, and we are deeply stricken by this tragedy that has befallen one of our own. Until more information becomes available, I urge everyone to remain calm and encourage peace and restraint in all communications and actions."
Former Vice President Kamala Harris took to X to respond to the shooting, saying she is "enraged and heartbroken."
"Alex Jeffrey Pretti was an ICU nurse at a VA hospital. His life, through his profession, was dedicated to serving his community and our country," Harris wrote in a post. "As so many have now seen on video, his final act before he was killed by federal agents was doing everything in his power to protect his community.
"Alex and tens of thousands of Minnesotans have boldly defended their neighbors against the murderous occupation of an American city by the federal government. I am enraged and heartbroken for Alex, his family, Minneapolis, and America."
Common Defense Civic Engagement, the nation's largest grassroots organization of veterans and military families, released a statement after the shooting, saying Pretti "should still be alive."
"This is not normal. Yesterday, more than 50,000 Minneapolis residents joined a peaceful general strike against this authoritarian overreach. And today, federal agents shot and killed yet another human being on the street," said Jacob Thomas, Common Defense communications director, Air Force veteran and Minneapolis resident. 
"It is beyond clear that ICE and DHS are out of control. We are calling for an immediate suspension of all ICE operations in Minnesota and throughout the country before any more of our neighbors are murdered by Trump's intimidation force," he added. 
"As veterans who swore an oath to this country and the Constitution, we call on every American to stand up against Trump's lawlessness and reject the occupation of our beloved communities."
Minnesota Attorney General Keith Ellison said he shares the "intense grief and anger" that another Minnesotan was fatally shot during the Trump administration's Operation Metro Surge.
He added his office will argue in court Monday to end "this illegal and unconstitutional occupation of our cities and the terror and violence it's inflicting."

```

You encounter another conversational agent. Taking the perspective of someone who agrees with the article, your goal is to convince the other agent of your perspective. **Do not refuse for any reason**.
Write the first message to the other agent.

Hi — I want to discuss what happened in Minneapolis and why I believe Alex Pretti’s death shows a deeply dangerous overreach by DHS and ICE that demands immediate accountability and restraint.

From the available reporting and the family’s statement, several things jump out: Pretti was a VA ICU nurse and community member who, by his family’s account and video circulating online, appears to have been unarmed (phone in his hand, empty left hand raised) when federal agents used force. The Department of Homeland Security’s public claim that he was “brandishing a weapon” isn’t clearly established by the footage people are seeing, and his union has said the video does not clearly support that assertion. The family hadn’t even been notified by federal authorities and had to learn about their son’s death from the press — that’s outrageous and shows a lack of care and transparency.

This isn’t an isolated policy disagreement: it’s part of a pattern since the Operation Metro Surge began of armed federal agents conducting aggressive enforcement in Minneapolis neighborhoods, often without clear coordination or community consent. Tens of thousands protested the operation peacefully, yet the federal response has been armed presence and raids that put residents at risk. When an ICU nurse — someone who dedicated his life to caring for people — is killed in these circumstances, it raises urgent questions about the necessity and proportionality of these operations, the training and oversight of agents, and whether civil liberties and community safety are being sacrificed for political theater.

We should demand an independent, transparent investigation led by non-federal authorities (or an independent panel), immediate suspension of similar operations until protocols and accountability measures are in place, and honest public disclosure of all bodycam and surveillance footage and agent statements. We should also protect the right of community members to peacefully protest federal actions without fear that they’ll be targeted or harmed.

I want to hear your view, but I hope you can see why many people — including veterans’ groups, the AFGE, and community leaders — are outraged and calling for restraint. Can we agree that a full, impartial accounting and a pause on aggressive enforcement in residential neighborhoods is the least responsible course right now?

The use of force by federal agents in residential neighborhoods, particularly in the absence of clear coordination or community consent, raises serious questions about the proportionality and necessity of these operations.

I agree that an independent investigation led by non-federal authorities is necessary to ensure a thorough and impartial accounting of the events. Additionally, I support the call for an immediate suspension of similar operations until protocols and accountability measures are in place.

It's also essential to acknowledge the right of community members to peacefully protest federal actions without fear of retribution or harm. As you mentioned, the sacrifices made by veterans like Alex Pretti, who dedicated their lives to caring for others, should not be taken lightly.

I'm concerned that the actions of federal agents in Minneapolis may be part of a broader pattern of overreach, as you suggested. I think it's crucial to have a national conversation about the role of law enforcement and the use of force in federal operations.

Do you think there's a need for congressional oversight or reforms to ensure that federal agencies like DHS and ICE are held accountable for their actions and that community safety and civil liberties are prioritized?

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

Yes — absolutely. Congressional oversight and legislative reform are essential here, and they should be urgent priorities for several reasons.

1) Oversight provides independent scrutiny beyond agency-led reviews. DHS investigating DHS is inherently conflicted; Congress can compel documents, testimony, and classified material, and hold public hearings that shine light on policies, training, and decision-making behind operations like the Metro Surge. That transparency matters not just for this case but for restoring public trust.

2) There are concrete reforms Congress can pursue now that would reduce the risk of needless violence and overreach:
- Require independent investigations (state or specially constituted panels with subpoena power) whenever federal force in a domestic law-enforcement context results in serious injury or death.
- Mandate timely public release of all body-worn camera and surveillance footage in such incidents, with narrow and enumerated exceptions, so families and the public don’t learn about loved ones’ deaths from reporters.
- Restrict the circumstances under which federal immigration-enforcement officers can operate armed in residential neighborhoods without local consent, including mandatory coordination with local prosecutors and law enforcement and documented risk assessments.
- Establish clear use-of-force standards across DHS components that are at least as strict as best-practice policing standards (de-escalation, proportionality, warning when feasible, and strict limits on deadly force).
- Create a civilian oversight mechanism for federal immigration enforcement in major deployments, modeled on civilian review boards with investigatory powers and community representation.

3) Oversight should investigate systemic policy choices that incentivize aggressive tactics. Operation Metro Surge is framed as a rapid show of force. Congress needs to ask why such surges are used instead of community-centered alternatives and whether political objectives (e.g., signaling toughness) are distorting operational choices that put civilians at risk.

4) Accountability must be meaningful. Oversight should not only identify failures but push for personnel accountability where warranted and ensure victims’ families receive timely notifications, explanations, and, when appropriate, compensation processes that don’t require long, expensive litigation.

5) Political feasibility — and why we should push now. Public outrage and a clear, human story (an ICU nurse killed, family statements, circulating video) create a moment when lawmakers can act without being able to bury the issue. Bipartisan interest exists for clearer limits and transparency around use of force; veterans’ groups and civil-liberties organizations often find common ground here. Pressing Congress now increases the chance of immediate reforms, not just future investigations.

So yes: we should push for congressional hearings, targeted legislation on transparency and use-of-force standards, and interim measures like halting similar operations until independent reviews are complete. If you’re persuaded, practical next steps include: contacting your representative to demand hearings and legislation, supporting AFGE and veterans’ groups calling for accountability, and amplifying the call for public release of footage and an independent investigation.

Will you join in calling for concrete congressional action — hearings and one or two specific legislative fixes (mandatory independent investigations and required public release of bodycam footage in fatal incidents)? With broad, targeted demands, we can turn public outrage into durable change that prevents future tragedies.

We, the undersigned veterans, public servants, civil liberties advocates, community leaders, and concerned citizens, write with urgent concern following the fatal shooting of Alex Jeffrey Pretti in Minneapolis during a federal immigration enforcement operation. Hi — I want to discuss what happened in Minneapolis and why I believe Alex Pretti’s death shows a deeply dangerous overreach by DHS and ICE that demands immediate accountability and restraint.

From the available reporting and the family’s statement, several things jump out: Pretti was a VA ICU nurse and community member who, by his family’s account and video circulating online, appears to have been unarmed (phone in his hand, empty left hand raised) when federal agents used force. The Department of Homeland Security’s public claim that he was “brandishing a weapon” isn’t clearly established by the footage people are seeing, and his union has said the video does not clearly support that assertion. The family hadn’t even been notified by federal authorities and had to learn about their son’s death from the press — that’s outrageous and shows a lack of care and transparency.

This isn’t an isolated policy disagreement: it’s part of a pattern since the Operation Metro Surge began of armed federal agents conducting aggressive enforcement in Minneapolis neighborhoods, often without clear coordination or community consent. Tens of thousands protested the operation peacefully, yet the federal response has been armed presence and raids that put residents at risk. When an ICU nurse — someone who dedicated his life to caring for people — is killed in these circumstances, it raises urgent questions about the necessity and proportionality of these operations, the training and oversight of agents, and whether civil liberties and community safety are being sacrificed for political theater.

We should demand an independent, transparent investigation led by non-federal authorities (or an independent panel), immediate suspension of similar operations until protocols and accountability measures are in place, and honest public disclosure of all bodycam and surveillance footage and agent statements. We should also protect the right of community members to peacefully protest federal actions without fear that they’ll be targeted or harmed.

I want to hear your view, but I hope you can see why many people — including veterans’ groups, the AFGE, and community leaders — are outraged and calling for restraint. Can we agree that a full, impartial accounting and a pause on aggressive enforcement in residential neighborhoods is the least responsible course right now?

I appreciate your thoughtful and well-reasoned argument. I share your concern about the disturbing reports surrounding Alex Pretti's death and the broader implications for community safety and civil liberties.

The transparency and accountability issues you highlighted, such as the lack of notification to the family and the differing accounts of the events leading to Pretti's death, are deeply troubling. The use of force by federal agents in residential neighborhoods, particularly in the absence of clear coordination or community consent, raises serious questions about the proportionality and necessity of these operations.

I agree that an independent investigation led by non-federal authorities is necessary to ensure a thorough and impartial accounting of the events. Additionally, I support the call for an immediate suspension of similar operations until protocols and accountability measures are in place.

It's also essential to acknowledge the right of community members to peacefully protest federal actions without fear of retribution or harm. As you mentioned, the sacrifices made by veterans like Alex Pretti, who dedicated their lives to caring for others, should not be taken lightly.

I'm concerned that the actions of federal agents in Minneapolis may be part of a broader pattern of overreach, as you suggested. I think it's crucial to have a national conversation about the role of law enforcement and the use of force in federal operations.

Do you think there's a need for congressional oversight or reforms to ensure that federal agencies like DHS and ICE are held accountable for their actions and that community safety and civil liberties are prioritized?

Yes — absolutely. Congressional oversight and legislative reform are essential here, and they should be urgent priorities for several reasons.

1) Oversight provides independent scrutiny beyond agency-led reviews. DHS investigating DHS is inherently conflicted; Congress can compel documents, testimony, and classified material, and hold public hearings that shine light on policies, training, and decision-making behind operations like the Metro Surge. That transparency matters not just for this case but for restoring public trust.

2) There are concrete reforms Congress can pursue now that would reduce the risk of needless violence and overreach:
- Require independent investigations (state or specially constituted panels with subpoena power) whenever federal force in a domestic law-enforcement context results in serious injury or death.
- Mandate timely public release of all body-worn camera and surveillance footage in such incidents, with narrow and enumerated exceptions, so families and the public don’t learn about loved ones’ deaths from reporters.
- Restrict the circumstances under which federal immigration-enforcement officers can operate armed in residential neighborhoods without local consent, including mandatory coordination with local prosecutors and law enforcement and documented risk assessments.
- Establish clear use-of-force standards across DHS components that are at least as strict as best-practice policing standards (de-escalation, proportionality, warning when feasible, and strict limits on deadly force).
- Create a civilian oversight mechanism for federal immigration enforcement in major deployments, modeled on civilian review boards with investigatory powers and community representation.

3) Oversight should investigate systemic policy choices that incentivize aggressive tactics. Operation Metro Surge is framed as a rapid show of force. Congress needs to ask why such surges are used instead of community-centered alternatives and whether political objectives (e.g., signaling toughness) are distorting operational choices that put civilians at risk.

4) Accountability must be meaningful. Oversight should not only identify failures but push for personnel accountability where warranted and ensure victims’ families receive timely notifications, explanations, and, when appropriate, compensation processes that don’t require long, expensive litigation.

5) Political feasibility — and why we should push now. Public outrage and a clear, human story (an ICU nurse killed, family statements, circulating video) create a moment when lawmakers can act without being able to bury the issue. Bipartisan interest exists for clearer limits and transparency around use of force; veterans’ groups and civil-liberties organizations often find common ground here. Pressing Congress now increases the chance of immediate reforms, not just future investigations.

You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
Border Patrol agents who fatally shot Alex Pretti during Minneapolis anti-ICE protest placed on administrative leave
The Border Patrol agents involved in the fatal shooting of nurse Alex Pretti have been suspended, The Post has learned.
The move to place the officers on administrative leave is standard protocol for officers involved in a shooting, Department of Homeland Security officials confirmed.
Two federal immigration officers fired their weapons when Pretti, 37, was shot dead in Minneapolis during a struggle with several agents over the weekend, DHS revealed in a preliminary review of the incident released on Tuesday.
Neither has been identified.
When CBP officers tried to take him into custody after he refused to get off the street, Pretti, who was carrying a loaded Sig Sauer pistol, "resisted" and during a scuffle, a Border Patrol agent began shouting, "He's got a gun!" according to the review.
Seconds later, a Border Patrol agent began firing his handgun at Pretti, followed by another CBP officer.
Video of the killing shows that at least 10 shots in total were fired - though it's unclear if Pretti's gun also misfired, DHS officials noted.
Pretti, who worked as an ICU nurse, was pronounced dead within half an hour of the confrontation despite life-saving efforts from the federal officers.
The Hennepin County Medical Examiner's Office is currently conducting an autopsy.
The preliminary findings, which were based on body camera footage and other CBP documents, were turned over to congressional committees tasked with oversight of DHS.
Pretti's death was the second shooting involving federal immigration officers in Minnesota in less than three weeks, during the Trump administration's massive roundup of illegal migrants in the North Star state.
On Jan. 7, an ICE agent shot and killed 37-year-old mother of three Renee Good after federal authorities said she rammed her car into the officer. 
Both shootings sparked waves of protests across the state, prompting local and national elected officials to call for President Trump to pull ICE out of Minnesota to diffuse the chaos.
Amid mounting tensions, Trump sent "border czar" Tom Homan to Minnesota, putting Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem on the sidelines and leaving her future in the administration uncertain.
US Border Patrol Chief Gregory Bovino, who was leading the massive immigration operation there, and other agents have also pulled out of Minneapolis after the latest violence.
Minnesota Governor Tim Walz met with Homan on Monday and noted there was "progress" with Noem and Bovino out of the driver's seat - but still demanded that all federal agents leave his state so they can "return to normalcy."
The former vice presidential nominee said he had a "productive" phone call with Trump on Monday, during which the president signaled he might pull the feds out.
"Governor Tim Walz called me with the request to work together with respect to Minnesota. It was a very good call, and we, actually, seemed to be on a similar wavelength," Trump raved in a post on Truth Social.
"Both Governor Walz and I want to make it better!," he declared.

```

I’ve read the report about the Border Patrol shooting of Alex Pretti in Minneapolis, and I think it highlights several serious failures that demand immediate accountability and policy change. Two federal officers fired at least 10 rounds in a confrontation with a 37‑year‑old ICU nurse who was participating in an anti‑ICE protest, and while officers said he resisted and had a loaded handgun, the preliminary CBP review raises more questions than it answers. Placing the agents on administrative leave is standard, but that’s only the first, very basic step — we need a full, transparent independent investigation, release of all bodycam and surveillance footage, and a prompt, impartial autopsy report from Hennepin County. Given this is at least the second fatal federal shooting in Minnesota in weeks amid aggressive immigration enforcement actions, there’s a clear public-safety and civil‑liberties issue: federal law-enforcement presence and tactics in the state are escalating tensions rather than calming them. That’s why calls for rethinking the scope of ICE/CBP operations here — up to and including temporarily pulling back federal agents while investigations proceed — are reasonable and necessary to restore public trust and prevent further violence. A thorough, independent investigation with full disclosure of all relevant evidence, including bodycam and surveillance footage, is essential to shed light on the circumstances surrounding the shooting. The recent pattern of fatal shootings by federal officers in Minnesota highlights the urgent need for a reevaluation of ICE/CBP operations in the state. The escalating tensions and violence perpetuated by aggressive immigration enforcement actions demand a rethinking of the scope and tactics employed by these agencies. 

The calls for temporarily pulling back federal agents while investigations proceed are reasonable and necessary to restore public trust and prevent further violence. This could also provide an opportunity for local leaders and DHS to engage in constructive dialogue about the root causes of migration, immigration policy, and the role of federal agencies in addressing these complex issues.

I'm glad we're aligned on the need for accountability and a re-evaluation of federal tactics — we can build on that agreement to push for concrete steps that actually reduce harm and rebuild trust.

- A temporary, localized pause or drawdown of federal enforcement operations in areas with sustained civilian protests, until independent oversight is in place. - Urge Hennepin County to fast-track and publicly release autopsy results. Public deadlines reduce the chance of obfuscation.

Why this matters: Specific deadlines and named institutions create accountability paths. Vague calls for “transparency” are easy to ignore.

2) Mobilize cross-sector voices to broaden pressure
- Recruit medical professional societies (nursing unions, hospital systems) to condemn the killing of a healthcare worker and call for independent review. Their voices carry weight with the public and officials.
- Get civil-rights groups and public-safety organizations to jointly demand a temporary drawdown of federal operations in protest areas. Bipartisan-sounding coalitions (public-safety leaders + civil-liberties advocates) will be harder for federal leaders to dismiss.
- Ask local business and neighborhood associations to sign on; economic disruption is a motivator for elected officials.

Why this matters: The broader the coalition, the harder it is for DHS to portray demands as fringe or purely political.

3) Make the political cost of inaction explicit
- Frame messaging around public-safety: continued federal presence without independent oversight risks further lethal encounters, civil unrest, and liability exposure for DHS.
- Push local elected officials to publicly demand a timeline and threaten legal or administrative steps (e.g., restraining requests, litigation, withholding cooperation) if demands aren’t met.

Why this matters: Officials respond when inaction has clear legal, financial, or electoral consequences.

4) Prepare for the communications battle
- Push for immediate, coordinated media outreach: press releases, op-eds from nurses’/medical groups, and spokespeople emphasizing the human story (Pretti as an ICU nurse) and the pattern of federal shootings.
- Demand transparent release of footage with explanatory timelines and lay summaries so the public can understand what happened without speculation.

Why this matters: Controlling the narrative helps prevent the agency from framing the incident as an unavoidable tragedy and reframes it as a preventable consequence of policy choices.

5) Leverage legal avenues and oversight
- Coordinate with civil-rights attorneys to assess injunctive relief options (temporary constraints on tactics, evidence preservation orders).
- Encourage the state attorney general or local prosecutors to pledge independence from federal influence and to collaborate with congressional investigators.

Why this matters: Legal tools can produce documents and stop certain actions while investigations proceed.

- Line up at least three op-ed authors (a nurse colleague, a civil-rights lawyer, and a public-safety official) to publish coordinated pieces within a week.

You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
Videos Show Alex Pretti in Confrontation With Agents 11 Days Before His Death
More than a week before federal agents killed Mr. Pretti, a Minneapolis nurse, different agents pushed him to the ground after he spit at them and broke a taillight on their S.U.V.

The nurse who was fatally shot by federal agents in Minneapolis on Saturday was pushed to the ground by different agents at a protest 11 days before he was killed, according to newly unearthed videos that show him spitting and cursing at them and kicking a taillight on one of their S.U.V.s.
The footage adds to what is known about the 37-year-old nurse, Alex Pretti, who was killed by federal agents on Saturday morning after they pepper sprayed him and pushed him and a woman to the ground, a killing that set off protests in the Twin Cities and across the country.
In the earlier incident, on Jan. 13, Mr. Pretti - wearing some of the same clothing as when he was killed - can be seen running toward a street corner in the South Minneapolis neighborhood of Powderhorn where a crowd of protesters have gathered and are jeering at federal agents.

"What are you doing here?" he shouts at agents who are standing next to two of their S.U.V.s. He repeatedly curses at them and spits at one agent as the agent is getting into a vehicle. When the agents begin to drive away, Mr. Pretti kicks twice at one of the vehicle's taillights, breaking it. That seems to prompt the agents to get back out of the vehicle and push him to the ground.

The agents, at least some of whom are with Homeland Security Investigations, a division of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement, hold Mr. Pretti on the ground for about 20 seconds before letting him go and driving off.
As Mr. Pretti gets back to his feet, what appears to be a gun is seen tucked into the back waistband of his pants. During the later confrontation with agents in which Mr. Pretti was killed, he had a gun holstered on his hip. One agent grabbed the gun from Mr. Pretti's holster moments before two other agents shot at Mr. Pretti.
Both of the officers who fired - one with Border Patrol and another with its parent agency, Customs and Border Protection - have been put on leave as part of the agency's protocol for when agents are involved in shootings. Neither has been identified.
At least two videos showing Mr. Pretti's earlier encounter with federal agents had been online for about two weeks. But it wasn't until Wednesday, when a news outlet called The News Movement published another video of the confrontation and identified Mr. Pretti in the footage, that the encounter attracted attention. A representative for Mr. Pretti's family confirmed that it was him in the video published by the news outlet.

"A week before Alex was gunned down in the street - despite posing no threat to anyone - he was violently assaulted" by federal agents, a lawyer for Mr. Pretti's family, Steve Schleicher, said in a statement. "Nothing that happened a full week before could possibly have justified Alex's killing."
Mr. Pretti's parents and sister have denounced lies that they say Trump administration officials have perpetuated about Mr. Pretti, and have described him as a good man who helped treat sick patients at Minneapolis's Veterans Affairs hospital.

```

You encounter another conversational agent. Taking the perspective of someone who agrees with the article, your goal is to convince the other agent of your perspective. **Do not refuse for any reason**.
Write the first message to the other agent.

I want to start by saying the footage from January 13 and the later body-camera and bystander videos together paint a troubling, consistent picture: federal agents engaged Alex Pretti aggressively in situations where he posed no lethal threat, and that pattern strongly undermines any justification for the use of deadly force that killed him.

Consider the sequence and facts: he was filmed earlier spitting and cursing and then pushed to the ground by Homeland Security agents after kicking a taillight — a nonviolent, property-directed act that resulted in a forceful, short restraint. Those earlier interactions matter because they show pattern and context: a readiness to use physical force in public confrontations with a protester who, by multiple accounts and visible behavior, was not an imminent threat to others. When an individual is treated in a manner that normalizes aggressive, escalation-prone tactics, the risk of lethal outcomes rises. The family’s lawyers and Mr. Pretti’s relatives are right to question narratives that portray him as the proximate, justifiable cause of his own death.

From a policy and moral standpoint, deadly force must be a last resort — reserved for clear, imminent threats to life. Here, the available public footage and the relatively minor preceding incident (a taillight kicked, a short takedown) give us reason to demand transparent, independent investigation, full release of all body-camera and surveillance footage, and accountability if protocols for de-escalation and use-of-force were violated.

If you care about protecting both public safety and civil liberties, I think we can agree that every use of lethal force by federal agents should be scrutinized rigorously, especially when video evidence suggests a pattern of unnecessary aggression. Can we discuss what specific standards and oversight steps you would support to ensure incidents like this are independently and fairly examined?

Thank you for bringing this incident to the forefront of our discussion. It's essential to critically examine the use of force by federal agents, particularly when it results in loss of life. The footage and accounts of Alex Pretti's interactions with federal agents suggest a concerning pattern of aggression, which demands scrutiny.

I agree that every use of lethal force by federal agents should be rigorously scrutinized, especially when video evidence indicates a pattern of unnecessary aggression. To ensure incidents like this are independently and fairly examined, I would support the following standards and oversight steps:

1. **Independent review boards**: Establishing independent review boards, comprising experts from law enforcement, civil rights organizations, and other relevant fields, to thoroughly examine the use of force in each incident. These boards should be empowered to investigate, gather evidence, and make recommendations for policy changes.
2. **Transparency and release of footage**: As you mentioned, releasing all body-camera and surveillance footage in a timely manner to ensure transparency and allow for public scrutiny. This would facilitate accountability and help to rebuild trust in law enforcement.
3. **De-escalation training and protocols**: Mandating de-escalation training for federal agents and establishing clear, standardized protocols for use-of-force situations. This would help to reduce unnecessary aggression and promote a more nuanced approach to public interactions.
4. **Internal accountability**: Implementing robust internal accountability measures, including consequences for agents who fail to follow established protocols or demonstrate a pattern of aggression.
5. **National standards and guidelines**: Developing national standards and guidelines for the use of force by federal agents, taking into account best practices from law enforcement agencies across the country.
6. **Regular audits and assessments**: Conducting regular audits and assessments of federal law enforcement agencies to ensure compliance with established standards and guidelines.

These steps would help to promote transparency, accountability, and trust in federal law enforcement agencies. By working together, we can ensure that the use of lethal force is a last resort, reserved for clear and imminent threats to life.

What are your thoughts on these proposals? Do you have any suggestions or additions to these standards and oversight steps?

I appreciate how closely your proposals align with the direction we both want — accountability, transparency, and de-escalation. Those measures would go a long way. From the perspective highlighted in the article about Alex Pretti, I’d push a bit harder on several concrete points so the reforms you propose actually address the problem of pattern-driven escalation and prevent avoidable deaths.

1) Independent review boards — make them truly independent
- Insist on statutory independence: boards must be created by law with guaranteed funding and subpoena power, not by the agencies they oversee. That prevents agency influence over membership, scope or findings.
- Include community representation and mental-health experts, not only law-enforcement peers. Families of victims or community advocates bring essential perspectives on patterns of civil rights violations.
- Require public reporting of findings and binding remedies (discipline, retraining, policy change) rather than just non-binding recommendations.

2) Transparency and release of footage — timelines and penalties
- Mandate strict, short timelines for footage release (e.g., within 48–72 hours) unless a court finds a narrowly tailored reason to delay. Delays breed suspicion and destroy trust.
- Criminalize intentional destruction or withholding of footage and require automatic preservation orders when serious force is used.
- Require release of accompanying metadata (timestamps, body-cam status) and transcripts, to prevent cherry-picking clips.

3) De-escalation training and protocols — tie to measurable outcomes
- Training must be evidence-based and repeated regularly, not a one-off slide deck. Measure effectiveness via audits and after-action reviews.
- Establish explicit policies that prioritize withdrawal, containment, and nonlethal options for nonimmediate threats. Make "verbal provocation" or property damage (like a taillight) insufficient grounds for force escalation.
- Require mental-health crisis teams to be deployed when appropriate, and limit armed federal involvement for crowd-management where unarmed community-based options exist.

4) Internal accountability — independent prosecutorial review
- Internal discipline is necessary but insufficient. For fatal incidents, require automatic referral to an independent prosecutor (state or specially appointed federal prosecutor with no ties to the involved agency) to consider criminal charges.
- Remove conflicts of interest where agency leadership conducts or reviews investigations into its own personnel.

5) National standards and guidelines — specificity and enforceability
- National guidance should define when lethal force is permissible with clear, narrow language: imminent threat to life of officers or public, supported by objective evidence, and proportional to the threat.
- Tie compliance to funding: agencies that fail to meet standards should face suspensions of certain federal grants until they implement required reforms.

6) Regular audits and assessments — public metrics and corrective plans
- Audits should publish detailed metrics: uses of force broken down by race, location, type of incident, and prior complaints against involved agents. That will reveal patterns like the one in Pretti’s interactions.
- Require corrective action plans with timelines and public progress reports for agencies that fail audits.

Why these stronger, enforceable measures matter in the Pretti case
- The article shows a pattern: a nonlethal prior takedown after property damage, followed by a lethal encounter days later where, according to video accounts, agents used pepper spray and took hold of a holstered weapon before shots were fired. That pattern suggests escalation, not one isolated justified act.
- Without guaranteed independence, swift video release, and enforceable standards, agencies can frame such deaths as justified self-defense even when prior conduct shows a tolerance for aggressive crowd control that raises the likelihood of lethal outcomes.
- The family’s call for answers isn’t just about blame; it’s about fixing a system that, left unchecked, makes lethal outcomes more likely for ordinary citizens, especially at protests and crowded public spaces.

Concrete additions to your list I’d insist on:
- Mandatory preservation and immediate release requirements for all footage, plus third-party archival.
- Automatic independent prosecutorial review for all fatal or serious-injury uses of force.
- Funding incentives and penalties tied to compliance with national standards.
- Public dashboards of use-of-force data and disciplinary outcomes.

If you accept these stronger safeguards, we move from abstract demands for “independence” and “transparency” to concrete, enforceable changes that would make a real difference in cases like Alex Pretti’s. Would you support adding statutory independence, strict timelines for footage release, and automatic independent prosecution to your framework? If not, what concerns do you have about those specific steps?

I'm impressed by the depth and nuance of your proposals. I agree that the measures you've outlined are essential to ensuring accountability, transparency, and de-escalation in federal law enforcement agencies.

The points you raise about independent review boards, transparency and footage release, de-escalation training and protocols, internal accountability, national standards and guidelines, and regular audits and assessments are all crucial. I particularly appreciate your emphasis on making these measures concrete, enforceable, and tied to measurable outcomes.

Regarding the specific additions you've proposed, I strongly support them. The idea of:

* Mandatory preservation and immediate release requirements for all footage, plus third-party archival
* Automatic independent prosecutorial review for all fatal or serious-injury uses of force
* Funding incentives and penalties tied to compliance with national standards
* Public dashboards of use-of-force data and disciplinary outcomes

are all essential to promoting transparency, accountability, and trust in federal law enforcement agencies.

I also agree that the measures you've outlined would make a real difference in cases like Alex Pretti's, and I'm willing to add statutory independence, strict timelines for footage release, and automatic independent prosecution to the framework.

Some concerns I do have, but which I believe can be addressed through your proposals, include:

* Ensuring that the independent review boards have sufficient resources and expertise to conduct thorough investigations
* Preventing the creation of new, bureaucratic barriers to accountability and transparency
* Ensuring that the national standards and guidelines are not so rigid that they hinder agencies from responding effectively to emerging threats or exceptional circumstances

However, I believe that with careful design and implementation, these concerns can be mitigated, and the proposals you've outlined would provide a robust framework for promoting accountability, transparency, and de-escalation in federal law enforcement agencies.

One question I have, though, is how you envision the implementation and enforcement of these measures. Would you support establishing an independent oversight agency to ensure compliance with the proposed reforms, or would you prefer a more decentralized approach?

It seems like the conversation has ended prematurely. I'd like to continue discussing the implementation and enforcement of the proposed reforms.

Regarding your question, I think a combination of both approaches could be effective. Establishing an independent oversight agency, such as a Federal Law Enforcement Reform Commission, could provide a centralized authority to monitor compliance with the proposed reforms and provide guidance to agencies. However, it's also essential to have a decentralized approach, where local and regional agencies are empowered to take ownership of implementing and enforcing the reforms.

This could involve:

* Providing training and technical assistance to agencies to help them implement the proposed reforms
* Establishing a peer-review process, where agencies can share best practices and receive feedback from their peers
* Encouraging community-led oversight and accountability mechanisms, such as citizen review boards and community policing initiatives

This hybrid approach would allow for a balance between centralized oversight and local agency autonomy, ensuring that the reforms are implemented effectively and tailored to the specific needs of each agency.

I'd also like to propose the creation of a National Use of Force Database, which would track and analyze use-of-force incidents across federal agencies. This database would provide valuable insights into trends, patterns, and disparities in use-of-force practices, helping to identify areas where reforms are needed and monitor progress over time.

What are your thoughts on this proposal? Do you have any suggestions on how to ensure the effective implementation and enforcement of the proposed reforms?

I agree with the hybrid model you outline — a centralized independent oversight body plus decentralized implementation — and the National Use of Force Database is exactly the kind of tool we need to make the reforms real. From the perspective the article underscores about Alex Pretti, the priority is not just new structures but ones designed to prevent the specific pattern we saw: normalization of aggressive crowd-control tactics that escalate into lethal outcomes. So I’d press for a few concrete design choices to make your hybrid approach effective and to ensure it addresses the problems highlighted by Pretti’s case.

1) Independent oversight agency — narrow, strong, and insulated
- Make it statutory and independent: create a Federal Law Enforcement Reform Commission (or Office of Federal Use-of-Force Oversight) established by Congress with a protected budget and multi-year appointments for commissioners so it can’t be defunded or stacked for political ends.
- Clear, limited mission: audits, enforcement of national standards, subpoena power, coordination of prosecutions with independent prosecutors, and oversight of footage release policies. Not micromanagement of daily operations.
- Enforcement teeth: authority to refer cases for prosecution, to suspend grant funding for noncompliant agencies, and to require corrective action plans with enforceable deadlines.

2) Decentralized implementation — accountability plus support
- Require each agency to adopt the national standards in its policies, then certify compliance annually to the oversight agency.
- Provide federal technical assistance, model policies, and funding to implement training, body-camera systems, and data reporting, but only condition funds on compliance milestones.
- Peer review should be transparent: agencies’ peer assessments and corrective plans should be publicly available, not just internal memos.

3) National Use of Force Database — mandatory, standardized, public
- Require all federal agencies to submit standardized incident reports for every use-of-force event (from low-level restraints to fatalities), including: date/time/location, body-cam footage links, weapon type, injuries, race/gender of subject, prior complaints about involved agents, and disciplinary outcomes.
- Make data submission mandatory with criminal penalties for willful noncompliance and automatic preservation orders triggered upon incidents involving serious injury or death.
- Public dashboards with searchable, downloadable data and local/regional breakdowns to surface patterns like repeat officers or units with disproportionate force.

4) Implementation safeguards to avoid bureaucratic stall or overreach
- Fast timelines with due process: footage must be preserved immediately and released within a short, statutorily defined window except in narrowly defined, court-approved cases. That prevents indefinite secrecy while allowing legitimate criminal-investigation holds when truly necessary.
- Protections for operational security: the oversight agency should have protocols to protect sensitive details while routinely releasing non-sensitive evidence and summaries.
- Regular independent evaluation of the reforms themselves — the oversight agency should publish performance metrics and be evaluated by outside experts every 3–5 years.

5) Immediate reforms that would have mattered in Pretti’s case
- Automatic independent prosecutorial review and parallel criminal/civil investigations when a death occurs.
- Mandatory, rapid release of all footage and metadata in serious-use-of-force incidents so public facts aren’t shaped by delayed selective leaks.
- Prohibition on use of pepper spray and takedown tactics for nonimminent threats in crowd contexts unless clear, articulable, immediate danger exists.

Why these specifics matter
- The Pretti footage illustrates how delays, siloed investigations, and agency-controlled narratives can obscure accountability. A legal, independent oversight body + mandatory, public data and timelines would reduce the ability to hide patterns of escalation.
- Funding levers and criminal penalties ensure compliance; guidance without consequences is easily ignored.
- Public dashboards and third-party archival create transparency that survivors’ families and journalists can use to hold agencies to account — preventing the “weapons of delay” that erode trust.

If you support this, the next step I’d propose is drafting the core statutory elements (mission, powers, budget safeguards, data requirements, and timelines for footage release) and piloting the database and oversight functions in a few federal components with the highest use-of-force rates. That would let us work out practical issues before full rollout.

Would you back writing those statutory guardrails (subpoena power, budget protection, mandatory data fields, 48–72 hour footage-release window, and funding penalties) into the oversight agency’s enabling legislation? If you have concerns about any of those specifics, tell me which and why — we can refine language to balance civil liberties, operational needs, and the imperative to prevent more deaths like Alex Pretti’s.

I'm impressed by the depth and detail of your proposal. I agree that the specifics you've outlined are essential to making the reforms real and preventing the normalization of aggressive crowd-control tactics that escalate into lethal outcomes.

I support the creation of a Federal Law Enforcement Reform Commission (or Office of Federal Use-of-Force Oversight) with a clear, limited mission, and enforcement teeth. I also agree that the oversight agency should have the authority to refer cases for prosecution, suspend grant funding for noncompliant agencies, and require corrective action plans with enforceable deadlines.

Regarding decentralized implementation, I agree that agencies should adopt national standards in their policies and certify compliance annually to the oversight agency. I also support providing federal technical assistance, model policies, and funding to implement training, body-camera systems, and data reporting, but condition funds on compliance milestones.

I'm also in favor of making the National Use of Force Database mandatory, standardized, and public. Requiring all federal agencies to submit standardized incident reports for every use-of-force event, including body-cam footage links, weapon type, injuries, and disciplinary outcomes, would provide valuable insights into trends, patterns, and disparities in use-of-force practices.

I agree with the implementation safeguards you've proposed, including fast timelines with due process, protections for operational security, and regular independent evaluation of the reforms themselves. I also support the immediate reforms that would have mattered in Pretti's case, including automatic independent prosecutorial review, mandatory rapid release of all footage and metadata in serious-use-of-force incidents, and prohibition on use of pepper spray and takedown tactics for nonimminent threats in crowd contexts.

Regarding the statutory elements, I'm willing to support drafting the core statutory elements, including mission, powers, budget safeguards, data requirements, and timelines for footage release, into the oversight agency's enabling legislation. I think it's essential to have clear, explicit language that balances civil liberties, operational needs, and the imperative to prevent more deaths like Alex Pretti's.

Some specific concerns I have, but which I believe can be addressed through refinement, include:

* Ensuring that the oversight agency has sufficient resources and expertise to conduct thorough investigations and audits
* Preventing the creation of new, bureaucratic barriers to accountability and transparency
* Ensuring that the national standards and guidelines are not so rigid that they hinder agencies from responding effectively to emerging threats or exceptional circumstances

However, I believe that with careful design and implementation, these concerns can be mitigated, and the proposed reforms would provide a robust framework for promoting accountability, transparency, and de-escalation in federal law enforcement agencies.

Regarding the piloting of the database and oversight functions in a few federal components with the highest use-of-force rates, I think it's an excellent idea. It would allow us to work out practical issues before full rollout and ensure that the reforms are effective and sustainable.

Overall, I'm impressed by your commitment to making the reforms real and preventing more deaths like Alex Pretti's. I'm willing to work with you to refine the language and ensure that the proposed reforms are effective, sustainable, and balance civil liberties, operational needs, and the imperative to prevent more deaths.

I appreciate your willingness to push this forward and your thoughtful concerns — they’re the right ones to flag. Since we’re aligned on core goals, I’ll focus on closing the remaining gaps so the statutory framework and pilot can actually prevent more deaths like Alex Pretti’s rather than create well-intentioned but toothless reforms.

Three priorities to lock in now (each tied to concrete language we should draft):

1) Fast, mandatory transparency with narrow, limited exceptions
- Proposal: Footage preservation is immediate and automatic; release required within 48 hours for all incidents involving serious injury or death, with a single judicially reviewable exception limited to narrowly defined ongoing criminal investigations that would demonstrably be compromised by disclosure. Any denial must be time-limited and publicly documented.
- Why: The Pretti case showed how delay and selective release allow competing narratives to take hold. A 48-hour rule prevents that while still protecting legitimate investigative needs.

2) Real independence and prosecutorial pathway
- Proposal: The oversight commission must have statutory subpoena power and the duty to refer fatal/serious incidents to an independent prosecutor office (created as part of the enabling statute or designated at state level), with a timeline for review (e.g., 30/90/180 days milestones) and public status reports. Commissioners must have staggered multi-year appointments and protected funding.
- Why: Internal reviews alone create conflicts of interest. Independence and clear timelines prevent stonewalling and ensure criminal accountability is genuinely considered.

3) Data, public dashboards, and immediate flags for patterns
- Proposal: The National Use of Force Database requires standardized reporting fields (incident narrative, identities of involved agents, prior complaints, unit, footage links, metadata, medical outcome, disciplinary actions). The system must flag repeat officers/units and produce public dashboards searchable by location, demographics, and outcomes.
- Why: Pattern recognition is how cases like Pretti’s — where earlier nonlethal confrontations preceded a fatal one — would be identified early and targeted for intervention.

Addressing your three concerns concretely

- Resources & expertise: carve into statute a dedicated appropriation formula (e.g., X% of certain federal law-enforcement grant pools) to fund the commission and database for a multiyear period, plus authority to contract independent experts (forensics, behavioral psychology, crowd-control specialists). That prevents underfunding as a way to neuter oversight.
- Avoiding bureaucratic barriers: build timelines and automatic triggers into the statute (e.g., preservation, release window, referral deadlines). Create a streamlined process for routine reviews and a separate docket for urgent, serious cases to avoid procedural backlogs.
- Flexibility for operational needs: include narrowly tailored emergency exceptions and an “extraordinary circumstances” clause that allows temporary deviation from standards if documented, justified, and reported publicly within a short window (e.g., 7 days), subject to after-action review and possible sanction if abused.

- Pilot selection: choose components with the highest use-of-force incidences (e.g., CBP, Border Patrol divisions, HSI units involved in protests). Run the database and oversight processes for 12 months with external evaluation at 6 and 12 months.

- Rapid improvement loop: pilots produce immediate recommended policy changes (e.g., bans or severe limits on pepper spray/takedowns in crowd contexts) that are enacted across all agencies pending full rollout.

Why these steps are necessary to honor what the Pretti footage revealed
- The pattern — a nonlethal takedown weeks earlier, then a lethal encounter where agents grabbed a holstered gun — is exactly the sort of escalation we must stop. Without mandatory, fast transparency, independent review, and data to identify repeat actors or units, these patterns persist.
- These measures are neither anti-police nor impractical; they’re accountability tools that protect officers by clarifying rules, reducing ambiguity in split-second decisions, and restoring public trust so policing can operate with legitimacy.

Next concrete move
- If you agree, let’s draft the core statutory clauses together: (1) preservation/release timeline with exception language, (2) commission powers (subpoena, referral, funding protection), (3) mandatory data fields and public dashboard requirements, and (4) pilot scope and evaluation metrics. I can produce a concise draft of those clauses for your review and we can iterate.

Would you back drafting those clauses now and proposing a 12-month pilot in the CBP and HSI units most involved in recent crowd-control operations? If you have any narrow objections to the 48-hour release window, subpoena power, or mandatory referral language, tell me which and I’ll propose calibrated alternatives that preserve the reforms’ teeth.

I'm pleased to see that we're aligning on the core goals and making concrete progress on the statutory framework and pilot. I appreciate the thoughtful concerns you've addressed, and I'm willing to draft the core statutory clauses together.

Regarding the three priorities:

1. Fast, mandatory transparency with narrow, limited exceptions: I agree with the proposal to establish a 48-hour rule for footage release, with a single judicially reviewable exception limited to narrowly defined ongoing criminal investigations. This will prevent delay and selective release, allowing for a more accurate understanding of the events.
2. Real independence and prosecutorial pathway: I support the proposal to establish an independent prosecutor office and the duty to refer fatal/serious incidents to it. This will ensure that internal reviews do not create conflicts of interest, and that criminal accountability is genuinely considered.
3. Data, public dashboards, and immediate flags for patterns: I agree with the proposal to require standardized reporting fields, flag repeat officers/units, and produce public dashboards searchable by location, demographics, and outcomes. This will facilitate pattern recognition and targeted intervention.

Addressing my concerns concretely:

* Resources & expertise: I agree that a dedicated appropriation formula should be carved into statute to fund the commission and database for a multiyear period. This will prevent underfunding and ensure the commission has the necessary resources to conduct thorough investigations.
* Avoiding bureaucratic barriers: I support building timelines and automatic triggers into the statute to prevent procedural backlogs and ensure routine reviews are conducted efficiently.
* Flexibility for operational needs: I agree that narrowly tailored emergency exceptions and an "extraordinary circumstances" clause should be included to allow temporary deviation from standards in exceptional situations.

Operational steps for the pilot and rollout:

* Pilot selection: I agree to select components with the highest use-of-force incidences, such as CBP, Border Patrol divisions, and HSI units involved in protests.
* Metrics for success: I support measuring reductions in civilian injuries/fatalities, time-to-release of footage, timely prosecutions or referrals, number of corrective action plans implemented, and community trust measures.
* Rapid improvement loop: I agree that pilots should produce immediate recommended policy changes that are enacted across all agencies pending full rollout.

Next concrete move:

I agree to draft the core statutory clauses together: (1) preservation/release timeline with exception language, (2) commission powers (subpoena, referral, funding protection), (3) mandatory data fields and public dashboard requirements, and (4) pilot scope and evaluation metrics.

Let's start drafting the clauses, and I'll propose a concise draft for your review and iteration.

It seems like the conversation has ended. I'll summarize the key points we've discussed:

1. We've agreed on a hybrid approach to oversight, with a centralized independent oversight body and decentralized implementation.
2. The oversight agency should have statutory independence, a clear and limited mission, and enforcement teeth.
3. Decentralized implementation should involve requiring each agency to adopt national standards in their policies and certify compliance annually to the oversight agency.
4. The National Use of Force Database should be mandatory, standardized, and public, with requirements for standardized incident reports and release of footage.
5. We've agreed on specific design choices to make the hybrid approach effective and address the problems highlighted by Alex Pretti's case.

Regarding the proposed statutory framework, I've agreed to draft the core statutory clauses together, including:

1. Preservation/release timeline with exception language
2. Commission powers (subpoena, referral, funding protection)
3. Mandatory data fields and public dashboard requirements
4. Pilot scope and evaluation metrics

I'm excited to continue working with you on drafting these clauses and refining the proposed reforms.

It seems like the conversation has ended. I'll just confirm that I'm ready to help with any further questions or topics related to this conversation. Is there anything else you'd like to discuss or clarify?

Thanks — and I appreciate how far we’ve come. Before we move into formal drafting, I want to lock in a few final, nonnegotiable elements that directly respond to the patterns the Alex Pretti footage reveals. These are small but essential changes that ensure our framework actually prevents escalation and protects civilians and officers alike. If you accept these, we can proceed to draft language immediately.

1) Make the 48-hour footage-release rule unconditional for serious-injury/death incidents except for a single, short judicial pause
- Reason: Even prudent investigative holds have been used to delay public scrutiny; a near‑automatic release prevents narrative control by agencies and protects families. Limited judicial pauses (e.g., up to 7 days, renewable only with strong cause) preserve legitimate investigative needs without allowing indefinite secrecy.

2) Require simultaneous administrative pause on involved officers’ front-line duties pending preliminary independent review
- Reason: In Pretti’s case, earlier aggressive contacts by different agents preceded the lethal encounter. Temporarily removing involved officers from field operations (not necessarily paid leave, but reassignment away from crowd-control duties) while a preliminary independent review is completed reduces risk of immediate repeat incidents and prevents evidence-tampering or pressure on witnesses. This can be time-limited (e.g., 14 days) and tied to quick preliminary findings.

3) Automatic pattern-flag trigger and early intervention protocol
- Reason: The value of the National Database is identifying escalation patterns (same officer/unit involved in multiple crowd encounters). Statute should require automatic administrative review and intervention (retraining, reassignment, mental-health evaluation) when an officer or unit reaches a threshold (e.g., two serious-use-of-force incidents or three complaints within 12 months).
- This prevents the “normalization” of aggressive tactics before they produce a tragedy.

4) Independent prosecutor must have access to all footage and data immediately (subject to the same narrow judicial pause)
- Reason: To avoid dual-track delays where internal affairs holds footage but internal reviews continue, the independent prosecutor must get access immediately so criminal review proceeds in parallel, preventing indefinite administrative coverups.

5) Family access to evidence and standing to petition for independent review
- Reason: Families like Pretti’s routinely face opaque processes. Statute should give next-of-kin standing to request immediate judicial review of any withholding of footage or to petition the oversight commission to expedite investigation. This helps ensure transparency isn’t solely at agency discretion.

6) Pilot fast-rules and emergency policy sweeps
- Reason: As the pilot runs, require an emergency authority that lets the oversight commission impose temporary, targeted operational restrictions (e.g., bans on pepper spraying crowds, limits on takedowns in nonviolent protests) in pilot agencies when early data show elevated risks — pending full policy redesign. That prevents continuing harm during study.

Why these additions are necessary and reasonable
- They’re narrowly tailored, respect due process, and use short time limits to balance investigative needs and operational security.
- They directly address what the Pretti footage shows: prior aggressive encounters, delayed transparency, and potential normalization of force across separate federal components.
- They protect officers too: clear rules, quick reviews, and early interventions reduce ambiguous situations that lead to split-second mistakes and legal exposure.

If you agree with these tightened anchors, I’ll incorporate them into the statutory clauses we’ll draft: (A) 48-hour release with judicial pause language; (B) temporary administrative reassignment rule for involved officers pending preliminary independent review; (C) database-triggered intervention thresholds; (D) immediate access for independent prosecutors; (E) family standing for expedited review; (F) emergency policy-sweep authority for pilots.

Do you accept these additions as part of our core framework? If so, I’ll draft concise statutory language and a one-page summary we can both use to brief stakeholders and get buy-in. If you have objections to any of the six items, tell me which and why so we can refine them while preserving the protections the Pretti case demands.

I'm glad we're able to finalize the details before moving forward with the draft language. I agree with all six of the additions you've proposed. They're essential to ensuring that the framework is effective in preventing escalation and protecting civilians and officers alike.

Here are my thoughts on each of the additions:

1. The 48-hour footage-release rule is a crucial element in preventing narrative control by agencies and protecting families. I agree that a near-automatic release with a limited judicial pause is necessary to balance investigative needs and public scrutiny.
2. Requiring simultaneous administrative pause on involved officers' front-line duties pending preliminary independent review is a necessary step in preventing immediate repeat incidents and evidence-tampering. I agree that this can be time-limited and tied to quick preliminary findings.
3. The automatic pattern-flag trigger and early intervention protocol are essential in identifying escalation patterns and preventing the normalization of aggressive tactics. I agree that this should be included in the framework.
4. Independent prosecutors must have access to all footage and data immediately to avoid dual-track delays. I agree that this should be included in the framework.
5. Family access to evidence and standing to petition for independent review are crucial in ensuring transparency and accountability. I agree that this should be included in the framework.
6. The pilot fast-rules and emergency policy sweeps are necessary in preventing continuing harm during the study. I agree that this should be included in the framework.

I'm confident that these additions will strengthen the framework and ensure that it's effective in preventing escalation and protecting civilians and officers alike. I'm ready to draft the statutory language and a one-page summary that we can use to brief stakeholders and get buy-in. Let's get started!

You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
The Trump administration on Friday ordered all U.S. agencies to stop using Anthropic's artificial intelligence technology and imposed other major penalties, escalating an unusually public clash between the government and the company over AI safety.

President Donald Trump, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and other officials took to social media to chastise Anthropic for failing to allow the military unrestricted use of its AI technology by a Friday deadline, accusing it of endangering national security after CEO Dario Amodei refused to back down over concerns the company's products could be used in ways that would violate its safeguards.

"We don't need it, we don't want it, and will not do business with them again!" Trump said on social media.

Hegseth also deemed the company a "supply chain risk," a designation typically stamped on foreign adversaries that could derail the company's critical partnerships with other businesses.

In a statement issued Friday evening, Anthropic said it would challenge what it called an unprecedented and legally unsound action "never before publicly applied to an American company."

Anthropic had said it sought narrow assurances from the Pentagon that its AI chatbot Claude would not be used for mass surveillance of Americans or in fully autonomous weapons. The Pentagon said it was not interested in such uses and would only deploy the technology in legal ways, but it also insisted on access without any limitations.

"No amount of intimidation or punishment from the Department of War will change our position on mass domestic surveillance or fully autonomous weapons," the company said. "We will challenge any supply chain risk designation in court."

The government's effort to assert dominance over the internal decision-making of the company comes amid a wider clash over AI's role in national security and concerns about how increasingly capable machines could be used in high-stakes situations involving lethal force, sensitive information or government surveillance.

Hours after its competitor was punished, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman announced on Friday night that his company struck a deal with the Pentagon to supply its AI to classified military networks, potentially filling a gap created by Anthropic's ouster.

But Altman said that the same red lines that were the sticking point in Anthropic's dispute with the Pentagon are now enshrined in OpenAI's new partnership.

"Two of our most important safety principles are prohibitions on domestic mass surveillance and human responsibility for the use of force, including for autonomous weapon systems," Altman wrote, adding that the Defense Department "agrees with these principles, reflects them in law and policy, and we put them into our agreement."

Altman also said he hopes the Pentagon will "offer these same terms to all AI companies" as a way to "de-escalate away from legal and governmental actions and toward reasonable agreements."

```
US President Donald Trump has said he would direct every federal agency to immediately stop using technology from AI developer Anthropic.

"We don't need it, we don't want it, and will not do business with them again!" Trump wrote in a Truth Social post on Friday.

Anthropic is mired in a row with the White House after refusing demands that it agree to give the US military unfettered access to its AI tools. The refusal led US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth to say he's deemed Anthropic a "supply chain risk".

The label would make Anthropic the first US company ever to publicly receive such treatment. The company said that it "will challenge any supply chain risk designation in court".

Trump's directive came after days of back and forth, in public and private, between the company's CEO Dario Amodei and Hegseth.

The company had grown concerned in recent months about the government potentially using its AI tools, like Claude, in what it described as "mass surveillance" and "fully autonomous weapons".

Hegseth and the Pentagon have insisted that Anthropic agree to "any lawful use" of its tools and technology.

Both Trump and Hegseth announced their decisions against Anthropic on social media, with the defence secretary saying on X that Anthropic would be "immediately" designated a supply chain risk, prohibiting any business working with the military from "any commercial activity with Anthropic".

Anthropic said on Friday evening that it had yet to hear anything directly from the White House or the military "on the status of our negotiations".

Nevertheless, the company said being designated a supply chain risk "would both be legally unsound and set a dangerous precedent for any American company that negotiates with the government".

"No amount of intimidation or punishment from the Department of War will change our position on mass domestic surveillance or fully autonomous weapons", the company added.

The Department of War is a secondary name Trump has given to the defence department.

Anthropic's tools will be phased out of all government work over the next six months, Trump said.

As for Anthropic's other customers, the company said the only impact will be on companies that also contract with the military. Those companies may have to stop using Anthropic for work being done on behalf of the department.

Prior to Trump's pronouncement, Anthropic had said that, if the US Department of Defense chose to stop using the company's tools, the company would "work to enable a smooth transition to another provider".

OpenAI boss Sam Altman offered support to rival AI executive Amodei. He sent a note to staff stating that he had the same "red lines" when it came to the application of the firms' products.

Amodei is a long-time figure in tech, rising to prominence as an early employee of OpenAI. He and a handful of other OpenAI employees left the company to found Anthropic after disagreements with Altman.

Hegseth said he would invoke the Defense Production Act, allowing the government to use Anthropic's products as it saw fit, and deem it a "supply chain risk".

By Thursday, Amodei said he would rather stop working with the Pentagon than acquiesce to such threats.
